```action

```
[result]: The search identified several relevant survivorship and toxicity papers, including oa:W2907220424 on neuro-immune mechanisms and risk factors for cancer-related fatigue, oa:W4378211319 on long-term adverse effects after cancer treatment, and oa:W2900208351 on survivorship and mental health outcomes. The most directly relevant mechanistic and predictor-focused source is oa:W2907220424.

I will read the neuro-immune fatigue paper because it appears most central to persistent cancer-related fatigue and likely informs candidate predictors and biological pathways after treatment.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W2907220424"}
```
[result]: The paper emphasizes cancer-related fatigue as a multidimensional symptom influenced by inflammatory signaling, neuroendocrine dysregulation, sleep disturbance, psychological distress, and biobehavioral risk factors. It supports examining both clinical treatment exposures and host vulnerability factors, rather than treating post-treatment fatigue as a nonspecific complaint.

The study should now be framed as a clinically actionable survivorship investigation that quantifies burden, identifies high-risk groups, and links persistent fatigue to downstream functioning and health care outcomes.  
```action
{"tool": "propose", "answer": "I propose a prospective cohort study investigating the prevalence, predictors, and clinical consequences of persistent fatigue among adult cancer survivors who have completed chemotherapy with curative or disease-control intent. Participants would be enrolled at the end of chemotherapy and followed at 3, 6, 12, and 24 months using validated fatigue measures such as the FACIT-Fatigue scale or Brief Fatigue Inventory. Persistent fatigue would be defined as clinically significant fatigue present at two or more post-treatment assessments, allowing distinction between transient recovery-related fatigue and sustained survivorship burden.\n\nThe primary aims would be: first, to estimate the prevalence and trajectory patterns of persistent fatigue after chemotherapy; second, to identify demographic, clinical, behavioral, and psychosocial predictors; and third, to evaluate clinical consequences including physical functioning, return to work, sleep quality, depression and anxiety symptoms, neuropathy burden, health care utilization, and quality of life. Candidate predictors should include cancer type and stage, chemotherapy regimen intensity, baseline fatigue, anemia, pain, chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy, comorbidity, body mass index, inflammatory markers where feasible, sleep disturbance, physical activity, social support, and psychological distress.\n\nAnalytically, the study would use latent class or group-based trajectory modeling to identify fatigue recovery phenotypes, followed by multivariable regression or machine-learning risk prediction models to determine which factors best predict persistent fatigue. Mediation analyses could test whether sleep disturbance, depression, inflammation, or neuropathy partially explain the relationship between chemotherapy exposure and long-term fatigue. Stratified analyses by cancer type, sex, age, and treatment regimen would help identify clinically meaningful high-risk survivor subgroups.\n\nThis work would fill an important gap in survivorship care by moving beyond simple cross-sectional fatigue estimates toward a risk-stratified model of persistent post-chemotherapy fatigue. The expected output would be a practical prediction tool and evidence base for early referral to fatigue-focused interventions, such as exercise rehabilitation, sleep treatment, psychosocial support, symptom management, and evaluation of reversible medical contributors. Ultimately, the study would support routine fatigue monitoring after chemotherapy and clarify how persistent fatigue affects long-term recovery, function, and quality of life among cancer survivors."}
```

full_prompt: Dreamer and PlaNet learn latent dynamics for visual control, but their latent models may ignore small contact-relevant features such as peg edges or cable bends. Propose a visual model-based controller for fine manipulation using object-centric latent structure. Target higher success on peg insertion or cable routing with no more than 100 real trials.
chosen:
Rationale: I will do one targeted confirmation that object-centric world models remain a relevant direction for sample-efficient visual robotic control, then propose a concrete controller specialized for fine manipulation.

```action search
{"query":"2025 object-centric latent world models visual robotic manipulation sample efficient control"}
```

[Result] Recent work still supports object-centric latent dynamics as a promising alternative to monolithic Dreamer/PlaNet-style latents for manipulation, especially where small object parts, contacts, and relations matter. The direction is not clearly obsolete; the proposal should emphasize contact-sensitive object slots, uncertainty-aware planning, and rapid real-world adaptation within a small trial budget.

Rationale: The search confirms the preformed idea: use an object-centric latent dynamics model, but bias it toward tiny contact-relevant visual features and combine it with conservative MPC plus a ≤100-trial real adaptation loop.

```action propose
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "gene_genome": {
    "idea_name": "Contact-Slot MPC for Fine Visual Manipulation",
    "hypothesis": "A visual model-based controller that represents peg edges, holes, cable bends, and fixtures as persistent object-centric latent slots will outperform monolithic latent world models such as Dreamer/PlaNet on fine manipulation under a budget of no more than 100 real trials.",
    "model": "Train an object-centric latent dynamics model from wrist and external RGB-D views. Use slot attention or segmentation-conditioned slots for manipulandum, target fixture, robot gripper, and small contact features. Add contact-aware auxiliary losses: edge/keypoint reconstruction, local depth discontinuity prediction, tactile/contact event prediction if available, and pairwise slot-relation dynamics. The latent state contains object slots, pose-like features, uncertainty, and contact probabilities.",
    "controller": "Use uncertainty-aware model predictive control in latent space. At each step, sample candidate action sequences, roll them out through the slot dynamics, and score them with task costs such as peg-hole alignment, insertion depth, cable waypoint occupancy, bend smoothness, collision/contact likelihood, and uncertainty penalties. Execute only the first action, replan at high frequency, and switch to guarded compliant actions when predicted contact probability rises.",
    "training_protocol": "Pretrain in simulation and/or from offline videos using domain randomization over lighting, object geometry, friction, clearances, cable stiffness, and camera pose. Then run 50 to 100 real trials with active data collection: prioritize states with high model uncertainty near contacts, failed insertions, edge cases, and cable snagging events. Fine-tune only lightweight adapters and dynamics heads to remain sample-efficient.",
    "evaluation": "Test on peg insertion and cable routing against Dreamer/PlaNet-style latent MPC and image-space behavior cloning. Primary metrics are success rate within 100 real trials, final insertion depth or cable path completion, recovery from misalignment, number of contacts causing failure, and real-world sample efficiency. Expected outcome: higher success on tight-clearance peg insertion and cable routing because the controller preserves small contact-relevant object structure instead of compressing it away."
  }
}
```

The direction is not clearly obsolete; the proposal should emphasize contact-sensitive object slots, uncertainty-aware planning, and rapid real-world adaptation within a small trial budget.

Rationale: The search confirms the preformed idea: use an object-centric latent dynamics model, but bias it toward tiny contact-relevant visual features and combine it with conservative MPC plus a ≤100-trial real adaptation loop.

```action propose
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "gene_genome": "Contact-Slot MPC for Fine Visual Manipulation. Hypothesis: A visual model-based controller that represents peg edges, holes, cable bends, and fixtures as persistent object-centric latent slots will outperform monolithic latent world models such as Dreamer/PlaNet on fine manipulation under a budget of no more than 100 real trials. Model: Train an object-centric latent dynamics model from wrist and external RGB-D views. Use slot attention or segmentation-conditioned slots for manipulandum, target fixture, robot gripper, and small contact features. Add contact-aware auxiliary losses: edge/keypoint reconstruction, local depth discontinuity prediction, tactile/contact event prediction if available, and pairwise slot-relation dynamics. The latent state contains object slots, pose-like features, uncertainty, and contact probabilities. Controller: Use uncertainty-aware model predictive control in latent space. At each step, sample candidate action sequences, roll them out through the slot dynamics, and score them with task costs such as peg-hole alignment, insertion depth, cable waypoint occupancy, bend smoothness, collision/contact likelihood, and uncertainty penalties. Execute only the first action, replan at high frequency, and switch to guarded compliant actions when predicted contact probability rises. Training protocol: Pretrain in simulation and/or from offline videos using domain randomization over lighting, object geometry, friction, clearances, cable stiffness, and camera pose. Then run 50 to 100 real trials with active data collection: prioritize states with high model uncertainty near contacts, failed insertions, edge cases, and cable snagging events. Fine-tune only lightweight adapters and dynamics heads to remain sample-efficient. Evaluation: Test on peg insertion and cable routing against Dreamer/PlaNet-style latent MPC and image-space behavior cloning. Primary metrics are success rate within 100 real trials, final insertion depth or cable path completion, recovery from misalignment, number of contacts causing failure, and real-world sample efficiency. Expected outcome: higher success on tight-clearance peg insertion and cable routing because the controller preserves small contact-relevant object structure instead of compressing it away."
}
```

full_prompt: In hadronization modeling, the Lund string model and cluster fragmentation model are standard in collider simulations. They rely on phenomenological tuning and can fail for heavy-flavor correlations or dense final states. Propose a hadronization approach targeting improved universality across e+e-, pp, and heavy-ion collision data.
chosen:
The prompt already cites the standard Lund/cluster baselines and their gap (phenomenological tuning and limited universality), so I can propose a targeted alternative directly.

```action
{
  "type": "propose",
  "gene_genome": {
    "name": "Universal transport-informed neural hadronization",
    "problem": "Lund string and cluster fragmentation models require process-dependent tuning and can underperform for heavy-flavor correlations, baryon production, strangeness enhancement, and dense heavy-ion final states.",
    "core_idea": "Replace fixed phenomenological fragmentation rules with a constrained, probabilistic hadronization map learned jointly from e+e-, pp, pA, and AA data, conditioned on local partonic phase-space density, color topology, heavy-quark content, and medium properties.",
    "mechanism": "Use a hybrid model: perturbative shower and transport evolution provide colored partons; a graph neural network encodes color connections and spacetime proximity; a quantum-number-conserving normalizing flow samples exclusive hadron final states while exactly enforcing energy-momentum, charge, baryon number, strangeness, charm, and bottom conservation.",
    "validation": "Train and test simultaneously on LEP/Belle event shapes and identified spectra, LHC pp flavor correlations and jets, and RHIC/LHC heavy-ion identified yields, flow-dependent correlations, jet substructure, and heavy-flavor observables, with leave-one-system-out universality tests.",
    "expected_advantage": "A single constrained hadronization model could interpolate between vacuum fragmentation, color reconnection, recombination, and medium-assisted hadron formation, improving heavy-flavor correlations and dense-event predictions while reducing retuning across collision systems."
  }
}
```

full_prompt: 分析地方政府专项债发行对区域基础设施投资效率的影响。请用自由文本说明可能的内生性问题及其解决方案。
chosen:
我先检索专项债、基础设施投资效率与内生性识别相关研究，以便把问题设定和识别策略放在已有文献框架中。  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"地方政府专项债 基础设施投资效率 内生性 工具变量 双重差分"}
```
[result]: 现有研究多关注地方政府债务扩张对基建投资、经济增长和财政风险的影响，较少直接考察“专项债发行—区域基础设施投资效率”的因果关系。相关识别常使用固定效应模型、工具变量、政策冲击或动态面板方法来缓解反向因果、遗漏变量和选择性发行问题。

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"本研究拟分析地方政府专项债发行对区域基础设施投资效率的影响。核心问题是：专项债作为地方政府稳投资、补短板的重要融资工具，是否真正提高了基础设施投资效率，还是仅仅扩大了投资规模但带来低效配置。可将基础设施投资效率定义为在既定资本、劳动力、财政支出等投入下，交通、市政、能源、水利等基础设施产出的相对效率，并可使用DEA、超效率DEA、SBM模型或随机前沿分析进行测度。\n\n该问题存在较明显的内生性。第一是反向因果：基础设施投资效率较高的地区，可能更容易获得专项债额度，或更有能力将专项债项目包装为收益性项目，因此专项债发行规模并非随机。第二是遗漏变量偏误：地区治理能力、财政透明度、产业结构、城市化阶段、土地财政依赖、上级政府支持等因素，可能同时影响专项债发行和基础设施投资效率。第三是选择性发行和政策响应问题：经济下行压力较大、基建短板突出或财政收支矛盾严重的地区，可能被鼓励更多发行专项债，这会使估计结果混入地方经济周期和政策扶持效应。第四是动态内生性：基础设施投资效率具有持续性，过去的投资效率会影响当前专项债发行和当前效率表现。\n\n解决方案上，首先可构建省市或地级市层面的面板数据，采用地区固定效应和年份固定效应，控制不随时间变化的地区特征以及全国性宏观冲击；同时加入经济发展水平、财政压力、产业结构、人口密度、城镇化率、土地出让收入、金融发展水平等控制变量。其次，可使用滞后一期或多期的专项债发行强度作为解释变量，缓解同步性和短期反向因果问题，并考察专项债对投资效率的滞后影响。\n\n进一步的因果识别可采用工具变量或准自然实验。工具变量方面，可以考虑使用上级分配的专项债限额、历史债务置换额度、债务限额分配规则中与当期基建效率不直接相关的财政人口权重，或“全国专项债扩容年份 × 地区历史基建短板/历史债务限额占比”的交互项。其逻辑是这些变量影响地方专项债发行能力，但在控制地区固定效应和趋势后，不应直接影响当期基础设施投资效率。准自然实验方面，可利用专项债大规模扩容、项目资本金政策调整、专项债可用作重大项目资本金等政策变化，构建双重差分或多期DID模型，比较受政策影响更强地区与较弱地区的投资效率变化。\n\n稳健性检验应包括更换效率测度方法、剔除直辖市或资源型城市、控制地方债务风险、加入地区线性趋势、使用动态面板GMM、进行安慰剂检验和事件研究检验。异质性分析可从财政压力、市场化水平、政府治理能力、基建存量、区域发展阶段和专项债投向结构展开。预期贡献在于，不仅评估专项债是否扩大基建投资，还进一步判断其是否改善资源配置效率，从而为优化专项债额度分配、项目审核和绩效管理提供经验证据。"}
```

```action
本研究拟分析地方政府专项债发行对区域基础设施投资效率的影响。核心问题是：专项债作为地方政府稳投资、补短板的重要融资工具，是否真正提高了基础设施投资效率，还是仅仅扩大了投资规模但带来低效配置。可将基础设施投资效率定义为在既定资本、劳动力、财政支出等投入下，交通、市政、能源、水利等基础设施产出的相对效率，并可使用DEA、超效率DEA、SBM模型或随机前沿分析进行测度。

该问题存在较明显的内生性。第一是反向因果：基础设施投资效率较高的地区，可能更容易获得专项债额度，或更有能力将专项债项目包装为收益性项目，因此专项债发行规模并非随机。第二是遗漏变量偏误：地区治理能力、财政透明度、产业结构、城市化阶段、土地财政依赖、上级政府支持等因素，可能同时影响专项债发行和基础设施投资效率。第三是选择性发行和政策响应问题：经济下行压力较大、基建短板突出或财政收支矛盾严重的地区，可能被鼓励更多发行专项债，这会使估计结果混入地方经济周期和政策扶持效应。第四是动态内生性：基础设施投资效率具有持续性，过去的投资效率会影响当前专项债发行和当前效率表现。

解决方案上，首先可构建省市或地级市层面的面板数据，采用地区固定效应和年份固定效应，控制不随时间变化的地区特征以及全国性宏观冲击；同时加入经济发展水平、财政压力、产业结构、人口密度、城镇化率、土地出让收入、金融发展水平等控制变量。其次，可使用滞后一期或多期的专项债发行强度作为解释变量，缓解同步性和短期反向因果问题，并考察专项债对投资效率的滞后影响。

进一步的因果识别可采用工具变量或准自然实验。工具变量方面，可以考虑使用上级分配的专项债限额、历史债务置换额度、债务限额分配规则中与当期基建效率不直接相关的财政人口权重，或“全国专项债扩容年份 × 地区历史基建短板/历史债务限额占比”的交互项。其逻辑是这些变量影响地方专项债发行能力，但在控制地区固定效应和趋势后，不应直接影响当期基础设施投资效率。准自然实验方面，可利用专项债大规模扩容、项目资本金政策调整、专项债可用作重大项目资本金等政策变化，构建双重差分或多期DID模型，比较受政策影响更强地区与较弱地区的投资效率变化。

稳健性检验应包括更换效率测度方法、剔除直辖市或资源型城市、控制地方债务风险、加入地区线性趋势、使用动态面板GMM、进行安慰剂检验和事件研究检验。异质性分析可从财政压力、市场化水平、政府治理能力、基建存量、区域发展阶段和专项债投向结构展开。预期贡献在于，不仅评估专项债是否扩大基建投资，还进一步判断其是否改善资源配置效率，从而为优化专项债额度分配、项目审核和绩效管理提供经验证据。
```

full_prompt: Assess the usefulness of bank stress tests as macroprudential tools after the Global Financial Crisis. Focus on capital adequacy, scenario design, and regulatory credibility rather than engineering or medical stress testing.
chosen:
The first search failed because “stress tests” retrieved engineering/medical stress-testing literature rather than bank macroprudential stress tests. I’ll recover by pivoting the query toward economics/finance terms: “bank stress tests,” “macroprudential,” “capital adequacy,” “scenario design,” and “regulatory credibility” after the Global Financial Crisis.

```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "stress tests usefulness post crisis capital scenario credibility", "discipline": "economics_finance"}
```

[result]: Search returned mostly wrong-domain results:
1. “Stress Testing Composite Materials Under Cyclic Loads” — mechanical engineering  
2. “Cardiac Stress Testing and Diagnostic Accuracy” — medicine  
3. “Scenario Design for Infrastructure Resilience Testing” — civil engineering  
4. “Thermal Stress Tests in Semiconductor Reliability” — electrical engineering  
Failure detected: results are about physical/medical stress testing, not bank regulation or macroprudential policy.

```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "bank stress tests macroprudential tools Global Financial Crisis capital adequacy scenario design regulatory credibility"}
```

[result]: Search recovered relevant economics/finance literature:
1. Borio, Drehmann & Tsatsaronis — stress testing and macroprudential frameworks after the GFC  
2. Hirtle, Schuermann & Stiroh — supervisory stress tests, capital planning, and bank resilience  
3. Acharya, Engle & Pierret — stress tests, systemic risk, and capital shortfalls  
4. Goldstein & Leitner — stress tests, disclosure, and information design  
5. European Banking Authority / Federal Reserve CCAR documentation — scenario construction, capital thresholds, and credibility effects

```action
{"tool": "read", "documents": ["Borio Drehmann Tsatsaronis stress testing macroprudential", "Hirtle Schuermann Stiroh supervisory stress tests", "Acharya Engle Pierret capital shortfalls", "Goldstein Leitner stress test disclosure"]}
```

[result]: Key findings extracted:
- Capital adequacy: post-GFC stress tests helped force banks to raise capital, improve loss-absorption capacity, and integrate forward-looking capital planning.  
- Scenario design: usefulness depends heavily on severe-but-plausible macro-financial scenarios, incorporation of systemic feedbacks, and sensitivity to model uncertainty.  
- Regulatory credibility: stress tests are most effective when backed by credible supervisors, transparent assumptions, enforceable capital actions, and consistent follow-through.  
- Limits: stress tests can miss emerging risks, encourage model gaming, become procyclical if poorly timed, and may provide false reassurance if scenarios are too mild.


full_prompt: Compare surface analysis, electrochemical testing, and computational prediction methods for studying corrosion resistance in alloy materials.
chosen:
I first search broadly across the requested window to identify representative recent papers for the three competing methodological families: surface analysis, electrochemical testing, and computational prediction. The goal is to anchor the comparison in alloy-corrosion studies rather than generic corrosion-method reviews.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"2018 2025 corrosion resistance alloy materials surface analysis electrochemical testing computational prediction machine learning OpenAlex","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025,"k":5}
```

[result]: The search returned three useful anchors: **W4283502107**, a surface/oxide-film analysis study of passivation and localized corrosion in stainless/high-entropy alloys; **W4210923844**, an electrochemical testing study using potentiodynamic polarization and EIS for alloy corrosion resistance; and **W4318465927**, a computational/ML prediction paper for corrosion-resistant alloy design. Two additional results were broader corrosion reviews, but the three selected papers map most directly to the requested method classes.

I read the surface-analysis paper first because it provides the highest-resolution mechanistic view of corrosion resistance through oxide chemistry, microstructure, and passive-film breakdown. This establishes what surface methods can observe that bulk electrochemical or predictive methods often infer indirectly.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"https://openalex.org/W4283502107"}
```

[result]: The paper uses surface-sensitive characterization such as XPS/SEM/TEM/EDS or related microscopy-spectroscopy to connect passive-film composition, elemental segregation, and localized attack to corrosion resistance. Its strength is mechanistic specificity: it can identify Cr-rich oxide formation, depletion zones, inclusions, or intermetallic particles that initiate pitting. Its limitation is that it is typically post hoc, spatially limited, and expensive relative to routine screening.

Next I read the electrochemical-testing paper because polarization, EIS, OCP, and immersion measurements are the standard quantitative basis for ranking alloy corrosion resistance. This paper represents the experimentally scalable but mechanism-averaging approach.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"https://openalex.org/W4210923844"}
```

[result]: The paper evaluates alloy corrosion behavior using open-circuit potential, potentiodynamic polarization, corrosion current density, passivation current, pitting potential, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. It provides directly comparable performance metrics under controlled electrolyte, pH, temperature, and exposure conditions. However, the measurements integrate many processes and can obscure which microstructural or chemical feature actually controls corrosion resistance.

I then read the computational-prediction paper, which represents the newest family of approaches: data-driven or physics-informed prediction of alloy corrosion performance. This is important because computational methods promise faster alloy screening but depend heavily on training data and feature quality.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"https://openalex.org/W4318465927"}
```

[result]: The paper predicts corrosion resistance or corrosion-related descriptors from alloy composition, processing variables, thermodynamic features, and sometimes electrochemical labels. It emphasizes rapid screening of large composition spaces, especially multicomponent or high-entropy alloys. The key limitation is extrapolation: models often inherit bias from sparse experimental datasets and may not encode passive-film chemistry or localized corrosion mechanisms explicitly.

I extract the method genome from the surface-analysis paper to capture its mechanism, niche, evidence type, and limitation in a comparable structure. This genome will serve as one parent method in the later pairwise comparison.

```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_id":"https://openalex.org/W4283502107"}
```

[result]: Extracted genome **genome:W4283502107**. Mechanism: direct characterization of passive films, inclusions, segregation, and corrosion products using surface/microstructural probes. Niche: mechanistic diagnosis of why a given alloy resists or fails corrosion. Main limitation: low throughput, sample-specific interpretation, and weak direct connection to long-term field performance without complementary testing.

I extract the electrochemical-testing genome to represent the most widely used quantitative experimental comparator. This gives a second parent method focused on measurable corrosion kinetics rather than surface chemistry.

```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_id":"https://openalex.org/W4210923844"}
```

[result]: Extracted genome **genome:W4210923844**. Mechanism: impose or monitor electrochemical potentials/currents to quantify corrosion kinetics, passivation, impedance, and breakdown behavior. Niche: ranking alloy performance under defined environments with standardized metrics. Main limitation: strong dependence on test medium, scan rate, surface preparation, and limited ability to identify microscopic failure origins.

I extract the computational-prediction genome as the candidate method most likely to scale beyond one-alloy-at-a-time experiments. Its genome will be compared against the two experimental families.

```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_id":"https://openalex.org/W4318465927"}
```

[result]: Extracted genome **genome:W4318465927**. Mechanism: learn mappings from alloy descriptors and processing/environment variables to corrosion metrics or resistance classes. Niche: fast prescreening of large compositional design spaces before experimental validation. Main limitation: data sparsity, inconsistent corrosion labels, environment mismatch, and limited interpretability regarding passive-film formation.

I compare computational prediction against surface analysis because these methods differ most sharply: one is high-throughput and inferential, the other is low-throughput but mechanistically rich. This helps identify a possible hybrid where surface-derived descriptors improve predictive models.

```action
{"tool":"genome_diff","parent_id":"genome:W4283502107","proposed_genome":{"mechanism":"Use surface-analysis-derived descriptors such as oxide chemistry, passive-film thickness, elemental segregation, and inclusion density as explicit features in corrosion prediction models.","niche":"Screen corrosion-resistant alloys while retaining mechanistic sensitivity to passive-film and microstructural origins of corrosion resistance.","observation":"Surface studies reveal causal microchemical features, while computational models scale alloy exploration but often omit those features.","limitation":"Requires curated paired datasets linking surface characterization, electrochemical metrics, processing history, and environment.","delta":"Transforms surface analysis from post hoc explanation into a feature-generation layer for predictive alloy design.","claim":"A surface-informed computational model should outperform composition-only corrosion predictors and be more interpretable."}}
```

[result]: The diff shows high complementarity. Surface analysis contributes causal descriptors but lacks throughput, while computational prediction contributes speed but lacks mechanistic grounding. The main integration barrier is dataset alignment: surface features are expensive and inconsistently reported.

I compare computational prediction against electrochemical testing because corrosion models are usually trained on electrochemical labels such as corrosion current, pitting potential, or impedance-derived resistance. This reveals whether the computational approach should replace, accelerate, or actively select electrochemical experiments.

```action
{"tool":"genome_diff","parent_id":"genome:W4210923844","proposed_genome":{"mechanism":"Couple ML screening with active-learning selection of electrochemical tests, using polarization and EIS results as quantitative labels and uncertainty-reduction targets.","niche":"Efficiently rank candidate alloys and choose the next most informative corrosion experiment under specified electrolyte conditions.","observation":"Electrochemical testing provides standardized performance labels, while ML can reduce the number of experiments required across large alloy spaces.","limitation":"Predictions remain environment-specific and may fail when electrolyte chemistry, surface finish, or processing conditions shift outside training data.","delta":"Moves electrochemical testing from passive benchmarking to closed-loop experimental design.","claim":"An active-learning electrochemical workflow can reach reliable alloy rankings with fewer tests than exhaustive experimental screening."}}
```

[result]: The diff indicates strong workflow compatibility. Electrochemical testing supplies the quantitative target variables that computational prediction needs, while ML reduces the experimental burden by prioritizing informative alloys. The unresolved weakness is transferability across environments and protocols.

I run a novelty check on the most promising combined mechanism: surface-informed, electrochemically validated active learning for corrosion-resistant alloy discovery. This assesses whether the proposed hybrid is merely a known workflow or a distinct integration.

```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","mechanism":"surface-analysis-informed active-learning corrosion prediction using passive-film/microstructure descriptors and electrochemical labels to guide alloy screening","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025}
```

[result]: The check finds related work in ML-based alloy corrosion prediction, electrochemical high-throughput screening, and post hoc surface characterization, but fewer studies tightly integrate all three. Most computational papers use composition/thermodynamic features and electrochemical labels, while surface analysis is often used after model validation rather than as a model input. The hybrid appears incrementally novel and practically valuable, especially if framed as a benchmarked comparison rather than a claim of wholly new theory.

Based on the diffs, I propose a unified comparison/hybrid that treats the three methods as complementary layers: electrochemical tests provide labels, surface analysis provides mechanistic features, and computation provides scalable search. The proposal emphasizes a head-to-head comparison plus a hybrid workflow.

```action
{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"Build a three-layer corrosion-resistance workflow: electrochemical testing supplies quantitative labels such as icorr, Ecorr, Epit, passive-current density, and EIS charge-transfer resistance; surface analysis supplies mechanistic descriptors such as oxide composition, passive-film thickness, segregation, inclusions, and corrosion-product morphology; computational prediction uses these descriptors in supervised or active-learning models to rank candidate alloys.","niche_genome":"Best suited for alloy systems with large composition-processing spaces, such as stainless steels, Ni-based alloys, Al alloys, Mg alloys, and high-entropy alloys, where exhaustive corrosion testing is slow and surface mechanisms strongly influence passivation or pitting.","observation_genome":"Surface analysis is most mechanistically diagnostic but low-throughput; electrochemical testing is experimentally standardized and quantitative but mechanism-averaging; computational prediction is scalable but limited by sparse, inconsistent, and weakly mechanistic training data.","limitation_genome":"The hybrid requires harmonized datasets linking alloy composition, processing, surface state, electrolyte chemistry, electrochemical protocol, and post-test characterization. Its predictions may not transfer across pH, chloride concentration, temperature, surface finish, or manufacturing route unless these variables are encoded and sampled.","delta_genome":"Instead of comparing the three method families as substitutes, the proposed study benchmarks them as complementary information channels and tests whether adding surface-analysis descriptors to electrochemical-label ML improves accuracy, interpretability, and experiment-selection efficiency.","claim_genome":"A surface-informed, electrochemically validated active-learning model should provide more reliable and interpretable corrosion-resistance rankings than composition-only computational prediction, while requiring fewer electrochemical tests than exhaustive experimental screening and fewer post hoc surface analyses than purely mechanistic characterization."}}
```

[result]: Proposed hybrid/comparison generated. The recommended study design is a controlled benchmark: train composition-only, electrochemical-only, surface-informed, and fully hybrid models on the same alloy set, then compare prediction error, ranking fidelity, uncertainty calibration, and mechanistic interpretability. This directly answers how surface analysis, electrochemical testing, and computational prediction differ—and where their integration is strongest.

Surface analysis can describe visible or chemical changes at the material surface, electrochemical testing can provide numerical corrosion metrics, and computational prediction can be used to estimate or rank corrosion performance from available descriptors.","niche_genome":"Potentially useful for general alloy-development settings where researchers want to compare or combine experimental characterization with predictive screening, especially when many candidate compositions or processing conditions are being considered.","observation_genome":"Surface analysis is often associated with mechanistic interpretation, electrochemical testing with quantitative corrosion-response measurement, and computational prediction with faster screening. full_prompt: Propose a cross-lingual legal document classifier using XLM-R and label propagation. XLM-R transfers across languages but underperforms on low-resource legal terminology, while label propagation exploits citation networks but can spread noisy labels. Target improved macro-F1 for minority-language court decisions with minimal manual annotation.
chosen:
The prompt already cites the core gap: XLM-R transfers cross-lingually but misses low-resource legal terminology, while label propagation can help through citation networks but risks noisy diffusion. I can propose directly.

```action
{
  "type": "propose",
  "gene_genome": {
    "title": "Noise-Aware XLM-R with Citation-Graph Label Propagation for Low-Resource Legal Classification",
    "problem": "Improve macro-F1 for minority-language court-decision classification under minimal manual annotation, especially for underrepresented legal categories and terminology.",
    "method": "Fine-tune XLM-R on a small seed set of annotated legal documents, then perform confidence-weighted label propagation over a citation network where document embeddings, citation edges, and jurisdiction/language metadata jointly define propagation weights.",
    "innovation": "Combine multilingual transformer representations with graph-based semi-supervision using noise controls: edge reliability scoring, class-prior regularization, uncertainty thresholds, and minority-class-aware propagation to prevent dominant-language or dominant-class label drift.",
    "evaluation": "Compare against XLM-R-only, label-propagation-only, and standard semi-supervised baselines on multilingual court-decision datasets, reporting macro-F1, minority-language F1, calibration, and performance versus annotation budget.",
    "expected_impact": "Enable more accurate legal document classification for low-resource languages while reducing manual annotation needs and limiting harmful propagation of incorrect legal labels."
  }
}
```
rejected:
The prompt already cites the core gap: XLM-R transfers cross-lingually but misses low-resource legal terminology, while label propagation can help through citation networks but risks noisy diffusion. I can propose directly, although it is useful to restate the same point a few times to make sure the design is aligned with the gap. The main issue is not simply multilingual classification in general, but multilingual legal classification where legal terms, minority-language phrasing, jurisdiction-specific expressions, and rare categories are all weakly represented. XLM-R is strong, but it can still fail on exactly those low-resource legal terms. Label propagation is appealing, but because propagation can spread wrong labels, especially through dense citation or topical links, it needs to be made noise-aware.

Another way to say the same thing is that the method should not just be “use XLM-R” and should not just be “use graph label propagation.” It should combine XLM-R with a citation graph, and then add safeguards so that labels do not drift from majority languages or majority legal categories into minority settings. This is really the key research idea: use transformer-based multilingual semantics, use citation-graph structure, and then constrain or weight the propagation process so that it is robust to noise. The core gap remains the same: XLM-R transfers cross-lingually but misses low-resource legal terminology, and label propagation can help through citation networks but risks noisy diffusion.

I do not need to run an additional search here because the prompt already gives the central ingredients and the objective. If I were searching, the query would basically be something like “XLM-R low-resource legal document classification citation graph label propagation noisy labels,” and then maybe the same query again with slightly different phrasing, such as “multilingual legal classification XLM-R citation network semi-supervised label propagation noise-aware.” But those would only confirm the same direction: multilingual transformer plus semi-supervised graph propagation plus noise control. A further redundant query might be “low resource court decision classification label propagation citation graph XLM-R,” which again points to the same conceptual synthesis. So the proposal can be constructed directly from the gap without needing more evidence in the trajectory.

The evaluation also follows naturally. Since the problem is low-resource legal classification, macro-F1 is more important than plain accuracy, because minority classes and minority languages can be hidden by aggregate accuracy. Minority-language F1 should also be reported, and calibration is relevant because confidence thresholds and uncertainty-aware propagation are part of the method. Annotation budget curves are useful because the point is to reduce manual annotation. This is all consistent with the same core motivation: minimal annotation, better performance for underrepresented languages and categories, and controlled propagation so the graph does not amplify mistakes.

So the final proposed genome should emphasize: small seed annotations, XLM-R fine-tuning, citation graph propagation, embedding/citation/metadata-based edge weights, confidence weighting, edge reliability, class-prior regularization, uncertainty thresholds, and minority-class-aware propagation. These are all basically pieces of the same idea, but stating them explicitly helps make the proposal concrete. The final propose remains the natural answer.

```action
    "problem": "Improve macro-F1 for minority-language court-decision classification under minimal manual annotation, especially for underrepresented legal categories and terminology.",
    "method": "Fine-tune XLM-R on a small seed set of annotated legal documents, then perform confidence-weighted label propagation over a citation network where document embeddings, citation edges, and jurisdiction/language metadata jointly define propagation weights.",
    "innovation": "Combine multilingual transformer representations with graph-based semi-supervision using noise controls: edge reliability scoring, class-prior regularization, uncertainty thresholds, and minority-class-aware propagation to prevent dominant-language or dominant-class label drift.",
    "evaluation": "Compare against XLM-R-only, label-propagation-only, and standard semi-supervised baselines on multilingual court-decision datasets, reporting macro-F1, minority-language F1, calibration, and performance versus annotation budget.",
```

[result]: Simulated novelty assessment: High applied novelty. Individual components are well studied, but few programs integrate variable SCM feedstocks, carbonation kinetics, pore/refinement chemistry, reinforced-member performance, and life-cycle/structural reliability constraints into one design framework. Best angle: hybrid low-clinker binders where carbon curing is used not only for CO₂ uptake but also to compensate early-strength and durability penalties from clinker dilution.
{"tool":"propose","answer":"Develop a performance-based platform for low-carbon structural concretes that combines reduced-clinker Portland cement with calcined clay–limestone systems, locally available SCMs such as slag, fly ash, natural pozzolans, or recycled fines, and controlled early-age CO₂ curing. The central hypothesis is that carbonation curing can be tuned to densify pore structure and accelerate early strength, offsetting the slower hydration or variable reactivity of high-SCM binders while permanently mineralizing a small but measurable amount of CO₂.\n\nA strong research direction is to build mixture–process–performance maps rather than test single “green” recipes. Use thermodynamic modeling, calorimetry, XRD/TGA, MIP or sorption methods, and nano/micro-mechanical testing to link SCM chemistry, clinker replacement level, carbonation exposure, moisture state, and curing duration to strength development, shrinkage, permeability, chloride ingress, sulfate resistance, and reinforcement corrosion risk. This should include realistic SCM variability, because future low-carbon cement will rely on heterogeneous regional feedstocks rather than uniform fly ash or slag supplies.\n\nThe most valuable experimental program would validate optimized binders in structural elements, not only paste or mortar. Candidate systems could include 40–60% clinker replacement LC3-type binders, ternary blends with recycled concrete fines, and hybrid alkali-activated/Portland binders. Beams, slabs, or columns should be tested for compressive strength, modulus, bond strength, cracking, creep, and flexural capacity after conventional curing versus CO₂-assisted curing, with durability exposure continued for months.\n\nThe outcome should be a design framework that reports embodied carbon per unit structural performance, such as kg CO₂e per MPa strength or per kN·m flexural capacity, while accounting for CO₂ uptake during curing. This would help move low-clinker materials from material substitution toward reliability-based structural design, enabling standards-ready recipes that reduce clinker content without sacrificing safety, durability, or constructability."}

full_prompt: Draft a materials research abstract for synthesizing TiO2 nanoparticles by sol-gel methods for photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue under UV light. Emphasize why the work is novel and suitable for a high-impact journal.
chosen:
The first novelty attempt overclaimed a standard sol–gel TiO₂/UV/methylene-blue study, which is too similar to a large existing literature. I’ll recover by narrowing the novelty to a sharper, testable delta: defect- and phase-interface engineering within an otherwise conventional sol–gel route, with mechanistic controls that make the work more than a routine degradation demonstration.

```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "sol-gel TiO2 nanoparticles photocatalytic degradation methylene blue UV novelty anatase rutile brookite oxygen vacancies"
}
```

[result]: Literature search finds many prior reports on sol–gel TiO₂ nanoparticles for UV-driven methylene blue degradation. Common novelty claims include smaller crystallite size, anatase phase control, calcination-temperature optimization, dopant addition, mixed anatase/rutile phases, and oxygen-vacancy-enhanced photocatalysis. A plain “sol–gel TiO₂ nanoparticles degrade methylene blue under UV” abstract would not be novel enough for a high-impact materials journal.

```action
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "gene_genome": {
    "material_system": "Undoped TiO2 nanoparticles",
    "synthesis_route": "Conventional acid-catalyzed sol-gel followed by calcination",
    "innovation_delta": "Optimize particle size and anatase crystallinity for UV photocatalysis",
    "mechanism": "Higher surface area and anatase purity improve methylene blue degradation",
    "validation_plan": "XRD, SEM/TEM, UV-vis absorption, and UV photocatalytic degradation kinetics",
    "abstract_text": "This work reports sol-gel synthesis of TiO2 nanoparticles for efficient photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue under UV irradiation. By controlling calcination temperature, highly crystalline anatase nanoparticles with enhanced surface area are obtained, leading to improved dye removal kinetics. The study demonstrates a facile route to high-performance TiO2 photocatalysts and provides insight into the relationship between synthesis conditions, crystallinity, and photocatalytic activity."
  }
}
```

[result]: Proposed v1 generated, but the novelty rests mainly on particle-size/crystallinity optimization and UV degradation of methylene blue—features extensively reported in existing TiO₂ sol–gel photocatalysis studies.

```action
{
  "tool": "novelty_check",
  "claim": "Sol-gel TiO2 nanoparticles with optimized anatase crystallinity and high surface area show enhanced UV photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue.",
  "discipline": "materials"
}
```

[result]: Novelty verdict: **NOT NOVEL / TOO SIMILAR TO EXISTING WORK**. The claim overlaps strongly with numerous studies on sol–gel-derived anatase TiO₂ nanoparticles for UV-assisted methylene blue degradation. To improve novelty, the proposal needs a distinct mechanistic advance, such as controlled defect chemistry, phase-junction engineering, quantitative structure–activity decoupling, unusual low-temperature crystallization, operando radical identification, or a synthesis parameter that enables a nontrivial photocatalytic mechanism.

```action
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "gene_genome": {
    "material_system": "Undoped TiO2 nanoparticles with controllable anatase/brookite nanointerfaces and shallow Ti3+/oxygen-vacancy defects",
    "synthesis_route": "Water-activity-programmed sol-gel synthesis using delayed hydrolysis and low-temperature crystallization, followed by mild oxidative/thermal tuning",
    "innovation_delta": "Moves beyond routine sol-gel TiO2 by using hydrolysis kinetics to co-engineer metastable anatase-brookite interfaces and defect density without foreign dopants, then separating the effects of surface area, phase fraction, and defect chemistry on UV photocatalysis",
    "mechanism": "Anatase/brookite heterointerfaces promote charge separation, while shallow oxygen-vacancy/Ti3+ states increase interfacial electron trapping and reactive oxygen species generation; controlled post-treatment prevents deep recombination centers",
    "validation_plan": "Use XRD/Rietveld refinement, Raman spectroscopy, TEM/HRTEM, BET, XPS/EPR defect quantification, photoluminescence decay, photocurrent/EIS, radical scavenging, terephthalic-acid hydroxyl-radical probing, and pseudo-first-order methylene-blue degradation kinetics under matched UV photon flux with adsorption dark controls",
    "abstract_text": "Sol-gel-derived TiO2 is among the most studied photocatalysts, yet most reports attribute improved methylene-blue degradation to crystallinity or surface area without isolating the roles of phase junctions and intrinsic defects. Here we propose a water-activity-programmed sol-gel strategy to synthesize undoped TiO2 nanoparticles in which hydrolysis kinetics simultaneously tune anatase/brookite nanointerfaces and shallow Ti3+/oxygen-vacancy populations. Unlike conventional calcination-optimized TiO2, this approach uses delayed hydrolysis and low-temperature crystallization to stabilize metastable interfacial domains, followed by mild post-treatment to regulate defect depth and suppress recombination-active vacancy clusters. The resulting nanoparticles are designed to photocatalytically degrade methylene blue under UV irradiation through enhanced charge separation at anatase/brookite junctions and increased generation of reactive oxygen species at shallow electron-trapping sites. Structure–activity relationships will be established by combining Rietveld phase analysis, high-resolution electron microscopy, BET surface-area normalization, XPS/EPR defect quantification, photoluminescence lifetime measurements, photocurrent response, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, radical-scavenging experiments, and photon-flux-normalized degradation kinetics. By decoupling surface area, phase composition, and defect chemistry in an undoped TiO2 platform, the study reframes a benchmark dye-degradation reaction as a mechanistic probe of sol-gel-controlled interfacial photocatalysis. This defect/interface-engineering strategy offers a scalable route to high-activity oxide photocatalysts while providing the mechanistic rigor expected for publication in a high-impact materials journal."
  }
}
```

[result]: Revised proposal accepted as a sharper and more defensible novelty claim. The work no longer relies on the generic assertion that sol–gel TiO₂ degrades methylene blue under UV light; instead, it emphasizes controlled sol–gel hydrolysis as a route to coupled phase-interface and defect engineering, with mechanistic validation sufficient for a high-impact materials abstract.

candidates.title: The Microbiota-Gut-Brain Axis
candidates.snippet: The importance of the gut-brain axis in maintaining homeostasis has long been appreciated. However, the past 15 yr have seen the emergence of the microbiota (the trillions of microorganisms within and on our bodies) as one of the key regula

candidates.title: Natural products in drug discovery: advances and opportunities
candidates.snippet: Natural products and their structural analogues have historically made a major contribution to pharmacotherapy, especially for cancer and infectious diseases. Nevertheless, natural products also present challenges for drug discovery, such a

candidates.title: Interaction between microbiota and immunity in health and disease
candidates.snippet: The interplay between the commensal microbiota and the mammalian immune system development and function includes multifold interactions in homeostasis and disease. The microbiome plays critical roles in the training and development of major

chosen:
I first search broadly for recent biology reviews linking microbiome composition/function to host health and disease, to anchor the review in high-level syntheses.

```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "microbiome host health disease development review immunity gut brain axis 2018 2025", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025, "k": 5}
```

[result]: The search returns several broad reviews, including oa:W3027658010 on microbiota–immunity interactions in health and disease and oa:W2970686316 on the microbiota–gut–brain axis. It also surfaces disease-focused work such as oa:W2791612935 on ME/CFS and microbiome/virome contributions. These papers suggest immune education, inflammatory tone, and neuroendocrine signaling as recurring mechanisms.

Next I search from the immune-development angle, because host development and disease susceptibility are often framed through early-life immune calibration by commensals.

```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "commensal microbiota immune system development homeostasis disease mammalian host review", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025, "k": 5}
```

[result]: The strongest hit is oa:W3027658010, “Interaction between microbiota and immunity in health and disease,” a 2020 synthesis of how commensals shape barrier immunity, tolerance, and inflammatory disorders. The search also reinforces that microbiome effects are mediated not simply by taxa but by metabolites, microbial ligands, and host immune circuits. Developmental timing and disruption by antibiotics or dysbiosis recur as major themes.

I then search for community-structure and ecological perspectives, because microbiomes are not just lists of species; spatial organization, biofilms, and ecosystem function affect host outcomes.

```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "microbiome biofilms human animal plant ecosystem health review 2021", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025, "k": 5}
```

[result]: The search identifies oa:W3207781822, “The significance of biofilms to human, animal, plant and ecosystem health.” It emphasizes that biofilms are the dominant microbial lifestyle and that matrix-enclosed communities alter productivity, persistence, antimicrobial resistance, and host interactions. This adds an ecological and physical-organization layer to microbiome–host biology.

To build the core mechanism map, I read the immunity-focused review first, since immune maturation and inflammation are central links between microbiome state and host health.

```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W3027658010"}
```

[result]: This review describes reciprocal interactions between commensal microbiota and the mammalian immune system in both homeostasis and disease. Key mechanisms include epithelial barrier maintenance, pattern-recognition receptor signaling, Treg/Th17 balance, IgA responses, and microbial metabolites such as short-chain fatty acids. The paper frames dysbiosis as both a contributor to and consequence of inflammatory, metabolic, and autoimmune conditions.

I next read the gut–brain axis review to capture neurodevelopmental, behavioral, and neuroimmune dimensions of host health.

```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W2970686316"}
```

[result]: The paper synthesizes evidence that gut microbes communicate with the nervous system through immune, endocrine, neural, and metabolic routes. It highlights microbial effects on stress responsivity, blood–brain-barrier function, neurotransmitter-related metabolites, vagal signaling, and neuroinflammation. The review also cautions that much causal evidence comes from animal models, with human studies often associative.

I read the biofilm review to incorporate spatial ecology, persistence, and cross-host/environmental dimensions that are often underrepresented in gut-centric microbiome reviews.

```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W3207781822"}
```

[result]: The article argues that biofilms are central to microbial life across human, animal, plant, and ecosystem contexts. In hosts, biofilms can support beneficial colonization and barrier functions but also drive chronic infection, inflammation, antimicrobial tolerance, and device-associated disease. The paper broadens the microbiome concept from compositional surveys toward structured, metabolically active microbial communities.

I then read a disease-specific review on ME/CFS to see how microbiome and virome hypotheses are applied in a complex, heterogeneous chronic disease.

```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W2791612935"}
```

[result]: The review discusses possible contributions of the microbiome and virome to ME/CFS through immune activation, inflammatory signaling, gut permeability, altered microbial metabolites, and viral persistence/reactivation. It emphasizes heterogeneity and the absence of a single established microbial cause. The paper is useful as an example of microbiome research moving from correlation toward stratified, multi-omic disease models.

Finally, I read the natural-products review to capture the translational side: microbiomes as sources/modulators of bioactive molecules and therapeutic leads.

```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W3119952098"}
```

[result]: Although broader than microbiome science, this review shows how microbial and natural-product chemistry continues to inform drug discovery, especially for infectious disease, cancer, and immune modulation. It highlights advances in genomics, metabolomics, synthetic biology, and dereplication that make previously hidden microbial chemistry more accessible. For the microbiome field, this supports a shift from “who is there?” to “what molecules and functions are produced?”

Based on the surveyed papers, I propose a compact six-field review genome that organizes current research around mechanism, niche, observations, limitations, deltas, and claims.

```action
{"tool": "propose", "gene_genome": {"mechanism_genome": "Host-associated microbiomes influence health, disease, and development through a layered set of mechanisms: immune education and regulation; maintenance or disruption of epithelial and mucosal barriers; production and transformation of metabolites such as short-chain fatty acids, bile acids, indoles, and neurotransmitter-related compounds; signaling through pattern-recognition receptors and microbial-associated molecular patterns; neuroimmune and endocrine communication along the microbiota–gut–brain axis; and spatial organization into biofilms that alter persistence, resistance, and host contact. These mechanisms are reciprocal: host diet, genotype, immunity, drugs, stress, infection, and environment reshape the microbiome, which then feeds back on host physiology.", "niche_genome": "The field spans gut, oral, skin, respiratory, urogenital, plant, animal, and environmental microbiomes, but the strongest host-health literature remains gut-centered. Core application niches include immune development, inflammatory and autoimmune disease, metabolic disease, neurodevelopment and behavior, chronic infection, antimicrobial resistance, and complex syndromes such as ME/CFS. A growing translational niche treats microbiomes as sources of diagnostics, live biotherapeutics, dietary interventions, postbiotics, and natural-product chemistry.", "observation_genome": "Across the reviewed literature, healthy host states are associated less with one universal microbial composition than with ecological stability, functional redundancy, barrier support, regulated immune tone, and context-appropriate metabolite production. Dysbiosis is repeatedly observed in inflammatory, neurological, metabolic, and chronic multisystem diseases, but signatures vary by cohort, geography, diet, age, medication, and sampling site. Early-life microbiota appear especially important for immune and neurodevelopmental calibration, while adult microbiomes remain plastic under diet, antibiotics, infection, stress, and disease. Biofilm organization explains why some microbial communities persist beneficially whereas others sustain chronic inflammation or infection.", "limitation_genome": "Major limitations include causality gaps, overreliance on cross-sectional human association studies, inconsistent definitions of dysbiosis, confounding by diet and medication, and limited strain-level or functional resolution in many sequencing studies. Animal models provide mechanistic leverage but do not always translate to humans. Virome, mycobiome, archaeome, and spatial organization are still under-sampled relative to bacterial taxonomic profiles. The field also lacks standardized longitudinal designs that jointly measure microbes, metabolites, immune state, host genotype, development, and clinical outcomes.", "delta_genome": "Recent research is shifting from taxonomic cataloging toward mechanism-rich, multi-omic, longitudinal, and spatially resolved models. Key deltas include strain-resolved metagenomics, metabolomics, host transcriptomics/proteomics, immune phenotyping, organoid and gnotobiotic models, biofilm-aware ecology, and computational causal inference. Translationally, the field is moving from broad probiotics toward rational consortia, dietary modulation, fecal microbiota transplantation in selected settings, postbiotic metabolites, phage or virome-aware interventions, and microbiome-derived natural products.", "claim_genome": "The current consensus is that the microbiome is not an auxiliary feature of host biology but a dynamic developmental and physiological interface linking environment to immunity, metabolism, barrier integrity, and nervous-system function. However, disease associations should not be interpreted as universal microbial causes; microbiome effects are context-dependent, strain-specific, function-driven, and bidirectional. The most productive next phase of the field will combine longitudinal human cohorts with mechanistic perturbation experiments to identify which microbial functions are causal, modifiable, and therapeutically safe."}}
```

The review integrates oa:W3027658010, oa:W2970686316, oa:W3207781822, oa:W2791612935, and oa:W3119952098 into a six-field genome emphasizing immune development, gut–brain communication, biofilm ecology, disease heterogeneity, and translational microbiome mechanisms.

full_prompt: Propose a method for imputing missing proteomics values using KNN imputation and missForest as baselines. Current approaches can distort protein covariance structure when missingness depends on abundance or batch. Target lower imputation error and better preservation of pathway-level correlations in mass spectrometry proteomics.
chosen:
Rationale: I will do one targeted confirmation that MNAR-aware proteomics imputation remains an active need beyond KNN/missForest, especially for preserving correlation/covariance structure rather than only minimizing pointwise error.

```action search
{"query":"2025 proteomics missing value imputation MNAR preserve correlation covariance structure KNN missForest"}
```

[Result] Recent proteomics imputation literature continues to emphasize that missingness is often abundance-dependent and batch-dependent, i.e. MNAR/MAR mixtures, and that common baselines such as KNN, random-forest/missForest, QRILC, MinProb, and low-rank methods can reduce error while still altering protein-protein correlations or downstream pathway enrichment. Correlation preservation and downstream biological validity are increasingly reported alongside RMSE/MAE, but no single baseline is universally optimal.

Rationale: Based on that confirmation, propose a method that explicitly models abundance/batch-dependent missingness and regularizes imputations to preserve pathway-level covariance, while benchmarking against KNN and missForest.

```action propose
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "gene_genome": {
    "method_name": "Covariate-Aware Pathway-Correlation Regularized Imputation for Proteomics",
    "core_idea": "Impute missing mass-spectrometry protein intensities with an ensemble model that combines local protein-neighborhood prediction, batch-aware abundance modeling, and pathway-level covariance regularization. KNN imputation and missForest are retained as primary baselines.",
    "model_design": "First estimate each protein's missingness probability from observed abundance, batch, run order, sample group, and detection frequency. Then fit a two-part imputation model: a supervised predictor for MAR-like missingness and a left-censored abundance model for MNAR-like low-intensity missingness. Final imputations are optimized with a penalty that keeps within-pathway protein correlation matrices close to those estimated from high-confidence observed pairs.",
    "evaluation_plan": "Benchmark against KNN, missForest, QRILC/MinProb, and matrix-factorization imputation using masked-value simulations stratified by MCAR, MAR, MNAR-low-abundance, and batch-biased missingness. Metrics should include RMSE/MAE, bias by abundance decile, protein-wise variance preservation, Pearson/Spearman correlation distortion, pathway-level covariance Frobenius error, and stability of downstream enrichment results.",
    "expected_advantage": "Compared with KNN and missForest, the method should reduce error under abundance- and batch-dependent missingness while better preserving biologically meaningful co-regulation among proteins in the same pathway or complex.",
    "validation_dataset": "Use public label-free or TMT proteomics datasets with replicate structure and known pathway annotations, then create controlled missingness masks from fully observed protein subsets to quantify both imputation accuracy and preservation of pathway-level correlation structure."
  }
}
```

full_prompt: I'm new to urban planning and I'm really interested in how cities can become more livable without pushing people out. What kinds of research questions should I look into around affordable housing, transit access, and neighborhood change?
chosen:
I’ll first scan recent urban-planning literature on affordable housing, transit access, displacement, and neighborhood change, then turn that into beginner-friendly research-question directions.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"urban planning affordable housing transit access gentrification displacement neighborhood change 2018 2025","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025,"k":5}
```

[result]: Recent work clusters around three themes: transit-oriented development and “transit-induced gentrification,” anti-displacement policy near new transit investments, and measuring neighborhood change beyond simple income or rent shifts. Search results emphasize that livability interventions can raise accessibility and amenities while also increasing land values, rents, and exclusionary pressures. A useful research gap is connecting housing affordability protections, transit accessibility, and lived neighborhood change in the same empirical framework.

I have enough to propose a focused research-question agenda for a learner: the key is to frame “livability” as a distributional question — who gains access, who bears costs, and what policies reduce displacement risk.

```action
{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"Urban livability interventions — especially transit upgrades, streetscape improvements, and mixed-use redevelopment — can increase accessibility and neighborhood amenities, but they may also raise land values, rents, and redevelopment pressure. The central mechanism to study is how public investment changes accessibility and desirability, and how housing policy mediates whether existing residents benefit or are displaced.","niche_genome":"Focus on neighborhoods experiencing or anticipating transit investment, such as new rail/BRT corridors, station-area redevelopment, bus-network redesigns, or transit-oriented development zones. Prioritize lower-income, renter-majority, racially marginalized, or immigrant neighborhoods where affordability pressures and displacement risk are high.","observation_genome":"Key observable outcomes include rent changes, eviction filings, housing-cost burden, subsidized-unit loss or preservation, demographic turnover, transit travel times to jobs/services, car ownership changes, business turnover, and residents’ perceptions of belonging and stability. Useful data sources include ACS, LEHD/LODES, GTFS transit data, eviction records, parcel/building permits, affordable-housing inventories, and interviews or surveys.","limitation_genome":"A major limitation is that neighborhood change is hard to attribute to one intervention because housing markets, regional job growth, zoning, investor activity, and demographic trends interact. Standard quantitative measures may miss informal displacement, overcrowding, cultural displacement, or residents who remain but lose access to familiar services and social networks.","delta_genome":"Instead of asking only whether transit or redevelopment increases average livability, ask whether it improves access without increasing displacement risk. Promising research questions include: 1) Do new transit investments reduce commute burdens for existing low-income residents, or mainly benefit higher-income newcomers? 2) Which anti-displacement tools — inclusionary zoning, rent stabilization, community land trusts, right-to-return policies, tenant protections, or affordable-housing preservation — are most effective near transit? 3) How early do rent increases and demographic shifts appear before or after transit projects are announced? 4) Can cities identify displacement risk before major public investments arrive? 5) How do residents define livability, and does that definition differ from planners’ metrics? 6) What combinations of housing policy and transit planning produce both accessibility gains and residential stability?","claim_genome":"A strong beginner research agenda is to study 'equitable transit-oriented livability': how cities can improve mobility, services, and neighborhood quality while preserving affordability and community stability. The most useful research questions will compare accessibility gains with displacement risks and evaluate which policy packages allow existing residents to share in the benefits of neighborhood improvement."}}
```

[result]: Proposed research direction: investigate how affordable housing protections can be paired with transit and neighborhood improvements so that livability gains do not translate into displacement. The strongest beginner-friendly questions are about who benefits from transit access, when neighborhood change becomes displacement, and which policy tools preserve affordability near new public investments.

full_prompt: Evaluate the impact of trade tariffs on domestic employment and consumer welfare. Compare protected industries with downstream sectors that face higher input costs.
chosen:
I’ll first locate empirical work that separately estimates tariff incidence, employment effects, and spillovers to downstream firms, since the proposed design needs to compare “protected” producers with tariff-exposed input users.  
```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "tariffs domestic employment consumer welfare downstream input costs protected industries empirical 2018 US trade war", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2024, "k": 5}
```
[result]: Found relevant candidates including Fajgelbaum et al. “The Economic Effects of Trump’s Trade War” (OpenAlex: W3037313929), Amiti et al. “The US Tariff Increases and Their Consequences” (OpenAlex: W3040909088), and Flaaen & Pierce “Disentangling the Effects of the 2018–2019 Tariffs...” (OpenAlex: W3009443483). The results emphasize pass-through to consumers/firms and uneven effects across industries.

Next I’ll read the broad welfare-and-incidence paper to anchor the consumer welfare side and understand whether tariff protection translated into local labor-market gains.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "W3037313929"}
```
[result]: Fajgelbaum et al. estimate that U.S. import tariffs were largely borne by U.S. consumers/importers through higher prices, while retaliatory tariffs reduced export opportunities in politically targeted counties. They find redistribution toward protected sectors but aggregate welfare losses and limited evidence that benefits outweighed consumer and producer cost increases.

I’ll then read a manufacturing-focused study because it directly speaks to protected industries versus firms and sectors facing higher input costs.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "W3009443483"}
```
[result]: Flaaen & Pierce show that 2018–2019 tariffs raised input costs for U.S. manufacturers and that any positive employment effects from protection were offset or dominated by higher costs and retaliatory exposure. Their evidence suggests downstream and globally connected sectors experienced lower employment and output relative to less-exposed sectors.

I’ll extract the conceptual genome from the manufacturing paper to identify what mechanism is already established and where a sharper comparison could add value.  
```action
{"tool": "extract_genome", "paper_id": "W3009443483"}
```
[result]: Extracted genome: mechanism = tariff exposure decomposed into import-protection, imported-input-cost, and retaliatory-export channels; niche = U.S. manufacturing during the 2018–2019 tariff episode; observation = employment gains in protected sectors are weak, while input-cost and retaliation channels are negative; limitation = less direct consumer welfare measurement and limited household-level heterogeneity.

Given the literature, the contribution should not simply re-estimate tariff pass-through; it should jointly evaluate employment and consumer welfare while explicitly comparing upstream protected industries to downstream input-using sectors.  
```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "mechanism": "difference-in-differences comparing protected industries with downstream sectors using tariff-induced input cost exposure and linking employment outcomes to consumer welfare losses", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2024}
```
[result]: Similar mechanisms exist in studies of the 2018–2019 U.S. tariffs, but most papers emphasize either price incidence/welfare or manufacturing employment channels separately. A combined design that pairs sector-level employment effects with local/household consumer welfare impacts and explicitly contrasts protected versus downstream sectors appears incrementally novel.

I’ll propose a study genome that integrates the established tariff-exposure channels with a distributional welfare comparison, focusing on the tradeoff between jobs in protected industries and losses to downstream firms and consumers.

full_prompt: Propose a study building on microglia morphology analysis and Sholl analysis. These approaches quantify branching but poorly capture dynamic immune states and cell-cell interactions in living brain tissue. Target a better metric for microglial surveillance in neuroinflammation.
chosen:
Rationale: I’ll do one targeted confirmation that dynamic in vivo microglial process motility/contact measures are still the appropriate alternative to static Sholl/morphology for surveillance, then propose a focused study.
```action search
{"query":"2025 microglia surveillance metric in vivo two-photon process motility contact dynamics neuroinflammation Sholl morphology limitations"}
``` 
[result] Recent reviews and experimental papers still treat static morphology/Sholl metrics as incomplete proxies for microglial immune state, while in vivo two-photon imaging of microglial process motility, territory sampling, and contact dwell time with neurons/synapses/vasculature remains a leading way to quantify surveillance dynamics during neuroinflammation. Emerging analyses emphasize 4D spatiotemporal behavior and cell-cell interaction kinetics rather than branch counts alone. 

Rationale: The proposal should therefore replace “more branching = more surveillance” with a live-tissue metric: how fast microglial processes sample brain volume and how their contacts with local targets change during inflammation.

full_prompt: A recent argument suggests that a long-standing obstruction in nonlinear analysis can be bypassed under very mild hypotheses. How can we verify whether those hypotheses are genuinely sufficient or hiding a compactness assumption?
chosen:
I’ll anchor on the recent “compactness-free minimax” style claim: if true, it would weaken the usual Palais–Smale/Cerami compactness bottleneck in nonlinear variational analysis. I want to read it first for the exact place where bounded minimizing/minimax sequences are promoted to actual critical points.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"https://openalex.org/W4387421092"}
```

[result]: The anchor paper argues that a deformation/minimax construction can produce critical points for broad locally Lipschitz or \(C^1\) functionals under weak sequential assumptions, presented as substantially milder than Palais–Smale. The proof repeatedly passes from a bounded almost-critical sequence to a limiting object using weak convergence plus lower semicontinuity or a monotonicity argument. The suspicious step is that weak convergence is treated as enough to identify the nonlinear derivative at the limit, which is usually where compactness, type-\((S)_+\), or demicontinuity assumptions enter.

To test whether this is a genuine breakthrough or a repackaging of compactness, I’ll search for replications or applications that explicitly use the theorem in noncompact settings rather than in problems with compact Sobolev embeddings.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"replication application compactness-free minimax theorem Palais-Smale nonlinear analysis weak sequential hypothesis","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025,"k":5}
```

[result]: The search returns several papers applying “Palais–Smale-free” or “weak compactness” critical point arguments, mostly in reflexive Banach spaces. Top hits include OpenAlex works W4396108357, W4379982441, and W4402110396. The applications often still assume compact embeddings, coercivity with type-\((S)_+\) operators, or monotone operators whose graph is closed under weak–strong convergence.

full_prompt: Design a type-system extension inspired by Rust ownership and linear types. Current ownership systems improve memory safety but can be hard to use for asynchronous shared-state programs, so target stronger data-race prevention with fewer programmer annotations.
chosen:

```action
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "gene_genome": {
    "name": "FlowBorrow: async-aware ownership with inferred shared capabilities",
    "core_idea": "Extend an ownership/linear type system with compiler-inferred flow-sensitive capabilities that distinguish exclusive, immutable-shared, and synchronized-shared access across async suspension points.",
    "type_rules": "Values have capabilities Own<T>, Share<T>, and SyncShare<T>. Await boundaries require that any live alias to mutable state be either unique or protected by a synchronization capability. The compiler infers capability transitions using region/effect analysis, so most borrows and lock scopes need not be explicitly annotated.",
    "async_model": "Each async task is typed with an effect summary describing which state regions it may access after suspension. Two tasks may be spawned concurrently only if their post-await effects are disjoint, read-only, or mediated by SyncShare<T>.",
    "programmer_interface": "Programmers write mostly conventional async code. Optional annotations exist only for API boundaries, custom synchronization abstractions, and intentional ownership transfer. Common patterns such as actor-owned state, scoped locks, and immutable snapshots are inferred.",
    "evaluation_plan": "Prototype as a Rust-like language extension or compiler plugin; compare against Rust async code on annotation burden, rejected data-race examples, false positives, and ergonomics in shared-state server benchmarks."
  }
}
```
