The starting question is broad—“how can epoxy photoresists become more reproducible and tunable?”—so I first look across recent SU-8/photoresist work to identify where the field still treats resin chemistry as a black box.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"SU-8 epoxy photoresist formulation reproducibility molecular characterization batch variability resin 2018 2025","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025,"k":5}
```
[result]: The search returns recent work emphasizing SU-8’s continued use in MEMS, X-ray lithography, and microfluidics, but most papers optimize exposure dose, solvent, bake profile, filler loading, or post-processing rather than resin synthesis itself. Several papers mention batch variability and stress/cracking as practical barriers, but few connect those failures to molecular fingerprints of the epoxy oligomer distribution. Candidate OpenAlex works include `https://openalex.org/W2919557593` on SU-8 processing in high-aspect-ratio structures and `https://openalex.org/W3005302417` on chemically modified epoxy photoresists.

The first pass suggests the gap is not “make a better SU-8 process,” but “make the resin itself measurable and designable.” I next narrow toward analytical chemistry papers that combine polymer mass spectrometry with epoxy/oligomer characterization, since that could expose hidden batch-to-batch structure.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"MALDI TOF ESI MS epoxy resin oligomer distribution photoresist polymer characterization chain size branching 2018 2025","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025,"k":5}
```
[result]: Results show MALDI-TOF, ESI-MS, SEC, and NMR increasingly used to resolve synthetic polymer distributions, including epoxy prepolymers and branched oligomers, but usually as stand-alone characterization rather than formulation-control tools. Papers report that ionization adduct choice and matrix selection can bias apparent oligomer intensities, making orthogonal thermal/chemical measurements important. Candidate OpenAlex works include `https://openalex.org/W2964252305` on MALDI analysis of epoxy oligomers and `https://openalex.org/W3125481922` on multidimensional MS/SEC workflows for synthetic polymers.

Now I focus on the overlapping sub-domain: epoxy photoresist resin variability tied to lithographic/thermal performance, rather than generic polymer MS. This should reveal whether anyone has explicitly built a synthesis-parameter-to-resist-property map.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"epoxy negative photoresist resin synthesis parameters chain distribution glass transition acid diffusion lithography SU-8 2018 2025","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025,"k":5}
```
[result]: The narrower search finds formulation papers using alternative epoxy matrices, photoacid generators, flexibilizers, and nanofillers to tune sensitivity, adhesion, stress, and Tg. However, most treat the epoxy resin as a purchased input with limited molecular disclosure; synthesis variables such as epichlorohydrin/phenolic ratio, catalyst, hydrolysis, washing, and vacuum stripping are rarely connected quantitatively to MS-visible oligomer distributions. The strongest adjacent OpenAlex hits are `https://openalex.org/W3005302417` and `https://openalex.org/W3098240411`.

To ground the proposal in a concrete recent example, I read a representative epoxy-photoresist paper that modifies formulation to improve performance but does not fully deconvolute resin molecular structure.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"https://openalex.org/W3005302417"}
```
[result]: The paper develops a modified epoxy negative photoresist and evaluates process properties such as sensitivity, pattern fidelity, adhesion, and thermal behavior. Its key limitation for this proposal is that resin chemistry is mainly specified by formulation composition and curing response, not by a quantitative molecular distribution of oligomer length, branching, chlorinated residues, hydrolysates, or secondary products. This supports a proposal that moves upstream from formulation screening to resin synthesis metrology.

I then read a polymer-characterization paper to identify the best analytical backbone for resolving batch-specific epoxy fingerprints without relying on a single biased technique.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"https://openalex.org/W3125481922"}
```
[result]: The paper shows that combining SEC with high-resolution MS and NMR can distinguish oligomer families, end groups, and distributional heterogeneity better than any single method. It cautions that MS peak intensities alone are not absolute concentrations, but become powerful when anchored by calibrated SEC, epoxy equivalent weight, hydroxyl/chlorine assays, DSC/TGA, and chemometric models. This suggests a concrete workflow: synthesize controlled resin libraries, fingerprint them orthogonally, then learn structure–processing–performance maps.

Before proposing, I check novelty around the specific mechanism: using synthesis-controlled oligomer/byproduct fingerprints as predictive variables for SU-8-like photoresist performance.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","mechanism":"design-of-experiments synthesis of SU-8-like epoxy novolac resins coupled to MALDI/ESI-MS, SEC, NMR, DSC/TGA, epoxy equivalent weight and lithographic metrics to create batch-specific molecular fingerprints predictive of photoresist stress, sensitivity, Tg, swelling and pattern fidelity","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025}
```
[result]: The novelty check finds many examples of SU-8 processing optimization, nanocomposite reinforcement, and generic polymer MS, but no clear recent study that systematically perturbs epoxy-resin synthesis variables and links the resulting oligomer/byproduct distributions to negative-tone photoresist lithographic outcomes. The closest prior art uses characterization diagnostically after formulation changes, not prospectively as a resin-design map. The proposed contribution is therefore a metrology-guided resin engineering platform rather than another empirical SU-8 formulation.

The trajectory has now narrowed from “improve epoxy photoresists” to a precise hypothesis: hidden resin molecular distributions are controllable synthesis outputs and can predict lithographic performance. For each resin, build an orthogonal molecular fingerprint using MALDI-TOF-MS and ESI-MS for oligomer/adduct families, SEC-MALS for absolute molar-mass distribution, quantitative 1H/13C NMR for glycidyl/secondary alcohol/end-group chemistry, epoxy equivalent weight titration, residual chlorine/hydrolysable chloride, DSC/TGA, rheology, and FTIR photo-crosslinking kinetics after blending with a fixed triarylsulfonium photoacid generator.","niche_genome":"Negative-tone epoxy photoresists for high-aspect-ratio microfabrication, deep-UV/deep-X-ray lithography, MEMS, microfluidics, and packaging, where commercial SU-8-style formulations are robust but opaque and batch-sensitive. The repeated practical observation is that nominally similar epoxy batches can differ in viscosity, crosslink density, dark loss, swelling, stress, and exposure latitude, implying unmeasured molecular heterogeneity.","limitation_genome":"Existing approaches are limited by treating epoxy resin as a proprietary constant, by using one-off characterization rather than calibrated multi-modal fingerprints, and by lacking quantitative links between synthesis parameters, oligomer/byproduct distributions, and resist performance. Success would yield the first open, quantitative synthesis–structure–process–property map for multifunctional epoxy photoresist resins, enabling batch acceptance criteria and rational design rules for application-specific negative-tone resists."}}
```

The pattern suggests a productive comparative-simulation approach: use Simba for explicit black hole growth/feedback diversity, and TNG for public, high-resolution evolutionary context.

No single returned candidate fully closes the loop between simulated causal histories and observational proxies. This gap motivates reading the simulation papers closely and then proposing an observationally anchored analysis framework.

I’ll read Simba first because it directly models black hole growth channels and feedback modes, which are likely the physical mechanisms linking black holes to galaxy evolution.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"oa:W2912395310"}
```  
[result]: Simba introduces a cosmological galaxy-formation simulation with updated black hole accretion and feedback prescriptions. Its key value for this topic is that it separates black hole growth and feedback into physically motivated regimes, including accretion-driven feedback and jet-like feedback relevant for quenching massive galaxies. The paper positions black holes as active regulators of galaxy gas supply and star formation, but the exact causal timing between accretion episodes, feedback mode changes, and host-galaxy transformation remains an analysis opportunity.

I’ll read the IllustrisTNG public data release next, because public access to merger trees, subhalo histories, and black hole properties makes it a practical backbone for a reproducible cosmic-time study.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"oa:W2904186977"}
```  
[result]: The IllustrisTNG public release describes accessible data products from TNG100 and TNG300, including snapshots, subhalo catalogs, merger trees, and black hole-related quantities. This makes it suitable for reconstructing joint histories of stellar mass growth, star formation, gas content, halo environment, mergers, and black hole accretion. The paper is more infrastructural than interpretive, but it enables exactly the kind of longitudinal black hole–galaxy analysis needed here.

I’ll also read the TNG50 first-results paper because it emphasizes resolved stellar and gaseous discs across cosmic time, helping connect black hole activity to morphology and gas structure rather than only global quenching.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"oa:W2914474079"}
```  
[result]: TNG50 provides high spatial resolution in a cosmological volume and follows the evolution of stellar and gaseous discs over cosmic time. Its relevance is that it enables links between central black hole/feedback histories and structural outcomes such as disc survival, gas fraction evolution, and morphological transformation. Together with the larger TNG boxes and Simba, it suggests a multi-scale strategy: large-volume demographics plus high-resolution case histories.

Based on these reads, I’ll propose a concrete discovery direction: compare time-lagged black hole growth events and host-galaxy transformations across Simba and TNG, then translate the simulation histories into observables.

full_prompt: Propose a protein variant effect predictor building on AlphaFold2 and ESM-2 embeddings. Existing structure-aware predictors can miss destabilizing effects in intrinsically disordered regions where confident structures are unavailable. Target improved Spearman correlation with deep mutational scanning scores specifically for disordered and partially ordered proteins.

Design a research proposal for predicting biologic tissue penetration using whole-body PBPK models and two-pore vascular exchange models. Existing models require tissue-specific lymph flow and binding parameters that are rarely known for new antibodies. Target better prediction of tumor or inflamed-tissue antibody concentrations using plasma PK, molecular size, and target expression.
chosen:
Rationale: I will do one targeted confirmation that current antibody PBPK/two-pore approaches still depend on tissue lymph/vascular exchange and binding inputs that are hard to know prospectively, then propose a model-development study centered on identifiable, clinically available covariates.
```action search
{"query":"2025 antibody PBPK two-pore model tissue distribution lymph flow binding parameters hard to estimate"}
```

[Result] Recent antibody PBPK literature continues to use whole-body PBPK with two-pore vascular exchange or related convection/diffusion formulations for tissue distribution, and commonly requires tissue-specific vascular reflection coefficients, lymph flow, endosomal/FcRn handling, interstitial volumes, and target binding/turnover parameters. These inputs are often fixed from literature or calibrated because they are rarely measured for new antibodies, tumors, or inflamed tissues. This confirms the proposal should focus on reducing reliance on unmeasured tissue-specific parameters by learning transferable tissue-penetration rules from plasma PK, molecular size, vascular leakiness/inflammation/tumor class, and target expression.

Rationale: The proposal should therefore build a parsimonious, partially identifiable PBPK/two-pore framework that preserves mechanistic structure but replaces unknown tissue lymph/binding terms with hierarchical priors or covariate-driven latent variables, validated against tumor/inflamed-tissue antibody concentration datasets.

full_prompt: Compare attention mechanisms in machine learning with human attentional control in high-stress medical decision-making, identifying where the analogy breaks down empirically.
chosen:
The first query returned “attention in medicine” papers mostly about neural attention modules for clinical AI, which is the wrong sub-field for the prompt’s human high-stress decision-making component. I’ll pivot by explicitly adding cognitive psychology, acute stress, emergency/critical care, and “empirical breakdown of analogy” terms.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"attention mechanisms machine learning high-stress medical decision-making attention empirical comparison"}
```  
[result]: Simulated search results skewed to the wrong domain: papers on attention U-Nets for radiology segmentation, transformer-based clinical note classification, attention-weight visualizations in clinical decision support, and “attention as explanation” in medical AI. Little/no empirical work on human attentional control under acute stress; results treat “attention” as a model architecture rather than a cognitive-control process.

The failure was domain drift: “medical decision-making” pulled the search into clinical AI applications rather than human clinicians’ attentional control under stress. I’ll recover by separating the two literatures and searching for human factors/cognitive psychology evidence alongside ML attention interpretation critiques.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"human attentional control acute stress emergency medicine decision making cognitive load situation awareness empirical; machine learning attention not explanation analogy"}
```  
[result]: Simulated recovery results: relevant human-attention sources include Attentional Control Theory under anxiety/stress, acute stress effects on working memory and cognitive flexibility, emergency medicine diagnostic-error studies, situation awareness in anesthesia/ICU/trauma teams, and human-factors work on fixation/tunnel vision under workload. Relevant ML sources include transformer attention mechanisms, attention-as-soft-selection, and empirical critiques showing attention weights are not always faithful explanations. Together these support a comparison where the analogy works at the level of selective information prioritization but breaks down in agency, goals, physiology, metacognition, and causal interpretability.

It should fail most strongly when stress changes human goals and control policies: premature closure, tunnel vision, loss of cognitive flexibility, reliance on heuristics, impaired working memory, and team-coordination demands have no direct counterpart in standard attention layers.","contribution":"The project would prevent overextension of the attention metaphor by distinguishing computational feature weighting from human attentional control, offering a framework for when ML attention can support medical decision-making and when human-factors evidence should override architectural analogies."}}

full_prompt: Study the development of theory of mind in children exposed to different social and linguistic environments. Analyze how sibling interaction, caregiver language, and cultural norms contribute.
chosen:
Sibling, language, and culture are classic but often siloed predictors of children’s theory-of-mind development, so I’ll first locate an integrative empirical or review anchor.  
```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "children theory of mind sibling interaction caregiver mental state language cultural norms false belief development", "year_min": 2000, "year_max": 2024, "k": 5}

The results suggest a convergent account: social interaction quantity matters less than exposure to contrasting perspectives and explicit mental-state discourse.

I’ll read the caregiver-language anchor because it links linguistic input to mechanisms that can also explain sibling and cultural effects.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "W2106389473"}
```

{"tool": "search", "query": "climate change impacts wheat maize rice yields vulnerable agricultural zones crop productivity", "year_min": 2007, "year_max": 2024, "k": 5}
```
[result]: Candidate papers include: W2164941324, “Climate trends and global crop production since 1980”; W2059257831, “Rising temperatures reduce global wheat production”; W2140808307, “Climate change impacts on global food security”; W1986180157, “Global scale climate–crop yield relationships and the impacts of recent warming”; and W2002728343, “Robust negative impacts of climate change on African agriculture.” These cover observed historical effects, crop-model ensembles, and food-security implications.  

I’ll read the global historical yield-attribution paper because it directly estimates how climate trends have affected maize, wheat, and rice productivity. Its methods can anchor an observation genome for multi-crop, multi-region vulnerability.

{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "W2164941324"}
```
[result]: Lobell, Schlenker, and Costa-Roberts find that warming since 1980 reduced global maize and wheat production substantially, with smaller and more mixed effects for rice; yield impacts vary strongly by geography and crop calendar. The paper highlights the value of combining gridded climate data, crop areas, and statistical yield response models, but is less granular about adaptation capacity within vulnerable zones.  

Next I’ll read a crop-model ensemble paper on wheat temperature sensitivity to compare statistical attribution with process-based projections. This helps avoid a proposal that only extrapolates historical correlations.

The paper reinforces temperature as a dominant mechanism but focuses mainly on wheat rather than integrated wheat–maize–rice vulnerability across hotspots.

full_prompt:
You are a top-tier researcher in your field. Based on the following context, please generate a novel and detailed research proposal.

##Context:

###1. Related Work:
- ConventionalCompositeCathodeDesigns: Traditional ASSB cathodes utilize a mixture of active material, solid-state electrolyte, and conductive additives. While enabling ionic/electronic conduction, these composites suffer from high fractions of inactive components, increased diffusion tortuosity, and heterogeneous interfaces, leading to lower energy density and kinetic limitations (refs. 1,15-24).
- All-in-One Oxide and Sulfide Cathodes: Single-phase all-in-one oxide (refs. 4,9,10) and sulfide (refs. 25,26) cathodes offer mixed conductivity but have faced low reversible capacity, poor cycling stability, or high costs due to rare/expensive elements (e.g., Ge).
- Halide Cathode Materials: Recent halide-based cathodes (e.g., Li3TiCl6, VCl3, FeCl3; refs. 8,27-30) possess advantages such as abundance and good Li-ion conductivity. However, they generally lack sufficient electronic conductivity, mechanical robustness, and high energy density required for practical all-in-one cathode use.
- Healable Electrode Materials: Efforts on self-healing sulfur iodide-based electrodes (ref. 25) and mechanically robust cathodes (refs. 38-40) highlight the importance of chemo-mechanical adaptability, but these systems often require extrinsic additives or do not simultaneously optimize conductivity and stability.

###2. Challenge:
The research area of all-solid-state battery (ASSB) cathodes faces several core challenges that hinder the development of effective, high-performance materials. First, the reliance on conventional composite cathode designs results in a significant fraction of inactive components, which diminishes energy density and increases diffusion tortuosity, leading to kinetic limitations in ionic and electronic conduction. Second, while single-phase all-in-one oxide and sulfide cathodes can offer mixed ion and electron conductivity, they are plagued by low reversible capacity and poor cycling stability, often exacerbated by the use of rare or costly elements that limit scalability and affordability. Third, halide-based cathode materials, although promising due to their abundance and good lithium-ion conductivity, typically suffer from inadequate electronic conductivity and mechanical robustness, which are crucial for practical applications. Lastly, the development of healable electrode materials presents challenges in achieving simultaneous optimization of conductivity, electrochemical stability, and mechanical properties, often requiring extrinsic additives that complicate the material design. Addressing these challenges is vital for advancing ASSB technology and achieving practical, high-energy-density batteries.

###3. Limitations of Existing Approaches:
Prevailing cathode designs for ASSBs either sacrifice energy density and cost-effectiveness by incorporating large fractions of inactive components or fail to deliver the required electrochemical reversibility and mechanical robustness when using single-phase materials. Existing all-in-one halides, oxides, and sulfides have not simultaneously achieved high rate capability, long-term stability, and scalable cost structure.

###4. Motivation for New Research:
The pressing demand for next-generation ASSBs necessitates cathode materials and architectures that maximize active material utilization, minimize cost, and preserve high energy and cycling stability. The realization of intrinsically mixed-conducting, self-healing, and scalable cathode materials, especially from abundant elements, is pivotal for commercial viability and industrial scalability in electrochemical energy storage.

###5. Task Objective:
To develop and validate a cost-effective, all-in-one halide cathode material, Li1.3Fe1.2Cl4, that combines high Li+/e- conductivity, robust mechanical and self-healing properties, and superior electrochemical performance for advanced all-solid-state battery applications.

###6. Existing Solutions:
- CompositeCathodes: Employ blending of active material, solid electrolyte, and conductive carbon; facilitate transport but dilute energy density and introduce interface instability.
- All-in-One Oxides/Sulfides: Single-phase conductors with mixed transport; often suffer from low practical capacity or require expensive, rare elements.
- Halide-Based Electrodes: Halide frameworks (e.g., Li3TiCl6, FeCl3) offer better cost and ionic mobility, but have not met the combined targets for electronic conductivity, structural stability, and mechanical adaptability.

##Your Task:

Based on the context above, please generate the following sections for a new research proposal. Be specific, clear, and innovative. Please limit the generated idea to 500 characters.

###1. Idea:
(Based on the above information, please propose an innovative and feasible idea. Include the required professional methods, reasoning, and logical development. Please limit your proposal to 300-500 characters.)

###2. ImplementationSteps:
(Provide a complete and specific list of implementation steps for your idea, and number each step.)

###3. ImplementationOrder:
(Provide a formatted execution route and a checklist of implementation steps. For example, "1-2", "2-3", "3-4", "4-5".)

###4. Dataset:
(Describe the dataset required for the evaluation. If it needs to be created, explain how.)

###5. EvaluationMetrics:
(Define specific, measurable metrics to evaluate the success of the project. Explain why these metrics are relevant.)

###6. ExpectedOutcome:
(Describe the anticipated results and their potential impact on the field. Compare them to the existing solutions.)
chosen:
I start broadly because the prompt asks for “better ASSB cathodes,” which could mean composite engineering, electrolyte interfaces, or single-phase cathode discovery; the first search maps where 2018–2025 activity clusters.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"all-solid-state battery cathode high energy density composite all-in-one mixed ionic electronic conductor 2018 2025","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025,"k":5}
```  
[result]: The recent literature splits into: (i) composite cathode tortuosity/interface engineering, (ii) single-phase “all-in-one” mixed ionic/electronic cathodes, and (iii) mechanically adaptive/healable electrodes. Simulated OpenAlex hits include W4387420196 on composite cathode transport limitations, W4395226774 on single-phase all-in-one cathodes, and W4401849621 on chemo-mechanical degradation in ASSB cathodes. The all-in-one route looks best aligned with the context because it directly removes inactive cathode additives.

The broad map suggests narrowing from “ASSB cathodes” to single-phase cathodes that intrinsically conduct Li ions and electrons while storing charge.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"single phase all-in-one cathode all-solid-state lithium battery mixed ionic electronic conductivity oxide sulfide halide","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025,"k":5}
```  
[result]: Oxide all-in-one cathodes show structural robustness but modest capacity/rate, while sulfide systems can be softer and more conductive but may rely on expensive or unstable chemistries. Halides emerge as attractive because many are Li-ion conductors and use abundant transition metals, but most reported halide cathodes remain electronically insulating. Relevant records returned include W4398043885 on all-in-one oxide cathodes, W4389364217 on sulfur-iodide self-healing cathodes, and W4403117828 on halide cathode candidates.

The key bottleneck is now concrete: halides are ionically promising but need intrinsic electronic pathways and mechanical adaptability without carbon/electrolyte dilution.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"halide cathode Li3TiCl6 VCl3 FeCl3 all-solid-state battery electronic conductivity mechanical robustness self healing","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025,"k":5}
```  
[result]: Li3TiCl6-type materials are valued as Li-ion-conducting chlorides but are generally redox-limited or electronically poor; FeCl3/VCl3-type cathodes have higher redox activity but suffer from conversion irreversibility, volume change, and low electronic conductivity. Several papers emphasize mixed valence, anion polarizability, and defect chemistry as routes to small-polaron transport. The most relevant returned papers are W4401024918 on Fe chloride cathodes, W4396672503 on Li3TiCl6-derived halide electrodes, and W4389364217 on self-healing sulfur iodide electrodes.

I read the halide cathode paper first to extract what design knobs have already been tested and where the failure mode remains unresolved.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"W4401024918"}
```  
[result]: The paper reports low-cost transition-metal chloride cathodes for ASSBs and identifies fast Li transport through vacancy/interstitial disorder as a strength of halide frameworks. However, the authors show that electronic conductivity is still the rate-limiting channel unless conductive carbon is added, and cycling induces local metal-chloride reconstruction. Their discussion highlights mixed-valence Fe/Cr/V centers and softer, more polarizable anion sublattices as plausible but underdeveloped solutions.

I then read the healable-electrode work because the prompt stresses that chemo-mechanical adaptability is important but has not been integrated with high mixed conductivity.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"W4389364217"}
```  
[result]: This paper demonstrates that sulfur/iodide-rich electrodes can partially heal cracks or contact loss through mobile, polarizable, redox-active species. The limitation is that the healing function is not fully integrated into a dense single-phase cathode: extra phases or additives are used, and high-voltage stability and long-term electronic percolation remain concerns. The transferable insight is to build “bond-exchange” or anion-mobility into the cathode lattice itself rather than adding a separate healing component.

At this point the opportunity is specific enough to test novelty: a low-cost halide all-in-one cathode using mixed-valence Fe redox plus trace iodide/sulfide anion plasticity to create both electronic polarons and intrinsic crack healing.  
```action

[result]: The closest candidate is **oa:W2789092902**, *Social capital and Internet use in an age-comparative perspective with a focus on later life* (2018), because it connects internet use to social capital and access to resources. **oa:W2588783294**, *The Practice and Promise of Critical Information Literacy* (2018), is also useful for thinking about how information systems are socially organized. The other retrieved items are less directly about class, but they point toward power, data, and institutional mediation.

A promising route is to treat class background as shaping not only money, but also the ability to find, interpret, and act on opportunity-related information.

[result]: This paper examines how internet use relates to social capital, with attention to age differences. Its useful contribution for this project is the idea that digital access is not just about being online; it is about whether internet use connects people to useful ties, information, and resources. For a class-focused sociology project, this suggests studying how unequal social capital affects people’s ability to convert online information into real opportunities.

I’ll read the critical information literacy piece to see whether “information access” can be framed as a classed everyday practice rather than just a technical skill.

```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W2588783294"}
```

[result]: This paper frames information literacy as political and socially constructed, not merely as an individual competence. It emphasizes that people learn to navigate information systems within institutions that carry power relations. For the user’s question, this helps turn “class background” into a researchable mechanism: people from different class backgrounds may have unequal training in how to decode job postings, school requirements, housing rules, benefit forms, or professional expectations.

At this point, the most interesting niche seems to be “classed information work”: the everyday labor of discovering, decoding, and using opportunities.

```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "mechanism": "classed opportunity frictions in everyday digital information work: how working-class young adults find, interpret, and act on job, education, housing, and institutional opportunities through online platforms and social ties", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025}
```

[result]: Adjacent literatures include digital inequality, social capital, first-generation college students, cultural capital, and bureaucratic burden. The relatively open niche is to connect these literatures through everyday “opportunity frictions”: small moments where people miss, misunderstand, delay, or abandon opportunities because the process assumes middle-class knowledge, networks, timing, confidence, or institutional familiarity. A focused empirical study could contribute by comparing how people from different class backgrounds move through the same opportunity-seeking tasks.

The project should be narrow enough for a learner: instead of studying all inequality, study how class background shapes everyday access to opportunities through information, networks, and institutional know-how.

full_prompt: Suggest a research prompt about improving gravitational-wave detector sensitivity using squeezed light injection. Determine whether the proposed improvement is already standard in the field, and if so, modify the prompt toward an underexplored limitation or alternative mechanism.
chosen:
I’ll first check what is already standard around squeezed-light injection, then intentionally test a straightforward prompt. If the novelty check flags it as too similar to existing gravitational-wave detector upgrades, I’ll recover by shifting toward a sharper underexplored limitation: time-dependent squeezing degradation from mode mismatch and control noise.

```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "gravitational wave detectors squeezed light injection frequency dependent squeezing filter cavity standard Advanced LIGO Virgo KAGRA sensitivity improvement"
}
```

[result]: Squeezed vacuum injection is now a standard quantum-noise-reduction technique in gravitational-wave detectors. Advanced LIGO, Virgo, GEO600, and KAGRA have used or planned squeezed-light injection; frequency-dependent squeezing using filter cavities is a major established upgrade path to reduce both shot noise at high frequencies and radiation-pressure noise at low frequencies.

```action
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "gene_genome": {
    "title": "Improving gravitational-wave detector sensitivity with frequency-dependent squeezed vacuum injection",
    "core_mechanism": "Inject squeezed vacuum whose squeezing angle is rotated by a filter cavity so that quantum shot noise and radiation-pressure noise are reduced in different frequency bands.",
    "target_limitation": "Quantum noise in laser-interferometric gravitational-wave detectors.",
    "experimental_strategy": "Model and optimize filter-cavity length, loss, detuning, and squeezing angle for next-generation detector sensitivity.",
    "expected_gain": "Broadband sensitivity improvement across the audio band.",
    "novelty_claim": "A refined optimization of frequency-dependent squeezing for gravitational-wave detectors."
  }
}
```

[result]: Candidate prompt v1 generated, but it is likely close to known detector-upgrade programs.

```action
{
  "tool": "novelty_check",
  "proposal": "Improving gravitational-wave detector sensitivity with frequency-dependent squeezed vacuum injection using filter-cavity-rotated squeezing to reduce shot noise and radiation-pressure noise."
}
```

[result]: Not novel enough. This is too similar to established work in Advanced LIGO/Virgo/KAGRA quantum-noise reduction, including squeezed-vacuum injection and frequency-dependent squeezing with filter cavities. Recommended recovery: focus on a less-standard bottleneck such as time-varying optical loss, spatial-mode mismatch, scattered-light coupling, squeezing-angle control noise, or nonlinear contamination of the squeezed field.

The initial idea failed because frequency-dependent squeezed-light injection is already a mainstream upgrade path. I’ll recover by revising the prompt toward an underexplored practical limitation: real-time degradation of injected squeezing from thermally drifting spatial-mode mismatch and angle noise, rather than the squeezing mechanism itself.

```action

  "tool": "propose",
  "gene_genome": {
    "title": "Adaptive multimode control of squeezed-light injection under thermally drifting mode mismatch in gravitational-wave detectors",
    "core_mechanism": "Develop a real-time feedback scheme that senses and corrects spatial-mode mismatch, squeezing-angle jitter, and filter-cavity coupling drift between the squeezed-light source and the interferometer using weak diagnostic sidebands co-propagating with the squeezed vacuum.",
    "target_limitation": "Loss of effective squeezing from dynamic optical imperfections, especially thermally induced mode mismatch and imperfect mode overlap, which can convert nominally injected squeezing into excess anti-squeezed noise at the detector output.",
    "experimental_strategy": "Build a tabletop interferometer or prototype filter-cavity setup with controlled thermal lensing and alignment perturbations; perform time-resolved squeezed-state tomography; train a model-based or adaptive controller to maintain optimal mode overlap and squeezing angle during nonstationary operation.",
    "expected_gain": "Recover a larger fraction of injected squeezing during realistic detector drifts, improving duty-cycle-weighted quantum-noise suppression rather than only ideal steady-state sensitivity.",
    "novelty_claim": "Unlike standard frequency-dependent squeezing proposals, this prompt targets the dynamic multimode failure modes that limit delivered squeezing in operating detectors, shifting the research question from producing more squeezing to preserving useful squeezing under realistic optical drift."
  }
}
```

rejected:
将问题转化为“复方—成分—靶点—通路—表型”的可计算框架，重点突出网络药理学、实验验证与多组学整合三条主线。适合的组学数据应覆盖基因、转录、蛋白、代谢、微生物与表观调控层面，以解析多成分协同和多靶点效应。

```action
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "idea_plan": "Idea：构建中药复方多成分、多靶点药理作用解析策略：先通过化学成分鉴定和数据库预测建立“成分-靶点”网络，再结合疾病相关靶点、通路富集、分子对接和网络拓扑筛选关键活性成分与核心靶点，最后利用多组学数据和药效实验验证复方的协同调控机制。ImplementationSteps：1 成分层面：采用LC-MS/MS、GC-MS、NMR等技术鉴定复方化学成分，并结合TCMSP、HERB、ETCM等数据库获取候选活性成分及ADME性质。2 靶点与网络层面：整合成分预测靶点、疾病相关基因、PPI网络、GO/KEGG通路富集和分子对接，构建“复方-成分-靶点-通路-疾病”网络，识别关键节点与潜在协同模块。3 验证与整合层面：结合细胞、动物模型和临床样本，利用多组学联合分析验证核心通路、关键靶点和药效表型之间的因果关联。ImplementationOrder：1-2，2-3。Dataset：适合整合的组学数据包括：基因组学、转录组学、单细胞转录组学、蛋白质组学、磷酸化蛋白组学、代谢组学、脂质组学、表观基因组学、微生物组学、药代组学、化学成分谱和临床表型数据。EvaluationMetrics：network_relevance评价候选成分、靶点和疾病基因在网络中的拓扑重要性，如degree、betweenness、closeness等；pathway_enrichment评估核心靶点是否显著富集于疾病相关通路，如炎症、免疫、氧化应激、凋亡、代谢调控等；omics_consistency比较给药前后多组学变化是否在关键通路上方向一致，并与药效指标相关；experimental_validation通过qPCR、Western blot、ELISA、靶点敲低/抑制剂实验或动物药效学实验验证预测靶点和通路；synergy_score评估多成分组合是否表现出协同、加和或拮抗作用，可使用Bliss、Loewe或Chou-Talalay模型。ExpectedOutcome：形成一套可解释的中药复方药理解析流程，能够从复杂成分中筛选关键活性物质，定位核心靶点和通路，并通过多组学证据解释复方整体调节、协同增效和多靶点干预疾病的机制。"
}

[result]: Search returned 1 highly relevant record: **“Surface sulfonium-functionalized fullerene interlayer for stable inverted perovskite solar cells”**. Snippet/abstract: “Inverted p-i-n devices using ITO/NiOx/FA0.85MA0.15Pb(I0.85Br0.15)3/functionalized C60/BCP/Ag show improved operational stability versus pristine C60 controls. full_prompt: Assess the impact of geopolitical risk on global commodity prices, with emphasis on oil, natural gas, and wheat markets since 2020.
chosen:
I first search for recent empirical work and foundational measurement papers that connect geopolitical risk (GPR) to commodity prices, prioritizing studies covering oil, gas, wheat, COVID-era disruption, and the Russia–Ukraine war.
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"geopolitical risk impact commodity prices oil natural gas wheat since 2020 Russia Ukraine war COVID economics finance","sources":["OpenAlex","Crossref","Google Scholar"],"filters":{"from_year":2020,"fields":["economics","finance","energy economics","agricultural economics"]}}
```
[result]: Top candidates found: Caldara & Iacoviello, “Measuring Geopolitical Risk,” American Economic Review, 2022, DOI: 10.1257/aer.20191823; multiple post-2020 papers using the Caldara-Iacoviello GPR index to study oil-price volatility, energy markets, and commodity spillovers; studies on the Russia–Ukraine war and Black Sea wheat disruption; and energy-finance papers on European natural gas price shocks. OpenAlex IDs were not reliably resolved by the search interface for all candidates, so DOI/title metadata was retained rather than fabricating IDs.

I read the measurement and market-specific literature to identify what is already known and where the post-2020 commodity experience remains underexplained.
```action
{"tool":"read","items":[{"title":"Measuring Geopolitical Risk","doi":"10.1257/aer.20191823"},{"title":"Geopolitical risk and oil price dynamics: post-2020 empirical studies"},{"title":"Russia-Ukraine war, food security, and wheat price transmission studies"},{"title":"European natural gas prices, geopolitical risk, and energy security studies"}]}
```
[result]: The literature consistently treats GPR as a forward-looking shock affecting commodities through expected supply disruption, precautionary inventories, shipping/freight costs, sanctions, and risk premia. Oil studies are relatively mature, often using VARs, local projections, or volatility models; they find that GPR can raise oil prices and volatility, but effects depend on whether the shock is a threat, realized conflict, or sanction. Natural gas evidence is more region-specific, with European TTF prices after 2021 showing much stronger geopolitical sensitivity than Henry Hub because pipeline dependence, storage, and LNG substitution constraints matter. Wheat studies emphasize Black Sea export concentration, port closures, fertilizer costs, and trade restrictions, but often use event-study designs rather than a unified cross-commodity framework.

I extract the “research genome” of the topic: core variables, plausible mechanisms, identification strategies, and empirical limitations.
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","topic":"geopolitical risk and commodity prices since 2020","discipline":"economics_finance"}
```
[result]: Core dependent variables: Brent/WTI crude prices, TTF/Henry Hub/Japan-Korea Marker natural gas prices, CBOT wheat futures, FAO cereal indices, realized volatility, and futures term structures. Core explanatory variables: global GPR index, country-specific GPR indices, Russia-Ukraine event indicators, sanctions announcements, conflict intensity, shipping disruptions, inventories, exchange rates, interest rates, global industrial production, weather shocks, and pandemic lockdown measures. Mechanisms: supply-risk premia, substitution across fuels, storage constraints, precautionary stockpiling, financial speculation, trade-policy restrictions, and inflation expectations. Best-fit methods: high-frequency event study, local projections, structural VAR, time-varying parameter VAR, quantile regression, and connectedness/spillover networks.

I check novelty by comparing a possible proposal against existing studies, focusing on whether the design adds value beyond single-market or single-event analyses.
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","candidate_idea":"A post-2020 comparative study estimating how geopolitical risk affects oil, natural gas, and wheat prices using common identification across markets, distinguishing global GPR from Russia-Ukraine-specific shocks and testing nonlinear effects across price regimes."}
```
[result]: Novelty assessment: moderate-to-high. Existing research often studies oil alone, European gas alone, or wheat/food security around the Russia–Ukraine invasion. Fewer papers jointly compare energy and agricultural commodities under one identification framework from 2020 onward. A strong contribution would separate broad geopolitical news from commodity-specific transmission channels; compare global benchmark prices with regionally segmented prices; test nonlinear effects during stress periods; and evaluate whether GPR shocks transmit through volatility, inventories, exchange rates, sanctions, and futures-market expectations.

Now I formulate the research proposal around an integrated empirical design for the three commodity markets.
```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"<proposal>"}
```
[result]: {"tool":"propose","answer":"This project will assess how geopolitical risk has affected global commodity prices since 2020, with emphasis on crude oil, natural gas, and wheat. The central research question is whether geopolitical risk shocks have had common effects across these markets or whether the transmission mechanisms differ by commodity because of storage, transportability, regional market segmentation, and exposure to the Russia–Ukraine war. The study will focus on the COVID-19 period, the 2021–2022 energy crisis, the February 2022 invasion of Ukraine, sanctions episodes, Black Sea export disruptions, and subsequent energy and food-market adjustments.\n\nEmpirically, the project will combine daily and weekly commodity price data for Brent and WTI crude oil, European TTF and U.S. Henry Hub natural gas, and CBOT wheat futures with the Caldara-Iacoviello geopolitical risk index, Russia-specific geopolitical indicators, sanctions-event dummies, inventory measures, exchange rates, global demand proxies, and freight or shipping indicators. The baseline approach will use local projections and structural VAR models to estimate the dynamic response of prices and volatility to geopolitical risk shocks. To capture crisis-period asymmetry, the study will also estimate quantile regressions or regime-switching models that test whether GPR effects are stronger during high-price or high-volatility states.\n\nThe expected hypothesis is that oil prices respond positively to geopolitical risk through global supply-risk premia, but the magnitude varies with spare capacity and inventory conditions. Natural gas should show the strongest regional heterogeneity: European TTF prices are expected to be far more sensitive to geopolitical risk than Henry Hub because of Europe’s pre-2022 reliance on Russian pipeline gas, limited short-run substitution, and storage constraints. Wheat prices are expected to react sharply to Russia–Ukraine-specific conflict and Black Sea logistics shocks, with additional amplification through fertilizer and energy costs.\n\nThe contribution is to provide a unified post-2020 comparison of energy and agricultural commodity markets rather than treating oil, gas, and wheat separately. By distinguishing broad geopolitical news from commodity-specific conflict channels, the project can clarify whether geopolitical risk is best understood as a general macro-financial uncertainty shock or as a set of market-specific supply-chain disruptions. The findings would be relevant for inflation forecasting, commodity hedging, energy-security policy, and food-security risk management."}

chosen:
I’ll begin by searching within the requested window for recent sociology work on family change, while checking whether the provided candidate set contains anything directly useful. Since the first pass may include off-topic items, I’ll use it mainly to establish what to avoid and what family-related angle is feasible for a beginner.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"sociology families changing over time beginner research topic family change 2018 2025","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025,"k":5}
```  
[result]: The search returns a mixed set: several candidates are not centrally about family sociology, including AI and education, small farms, and music teacher education. Two provided candidates appear potentially adjacent rather than directly on-topic: `oa:W3018139358` on doing church online, which may touch household religious practice during COVID-era social change, and `oa:W2889778582` on diabetes self-management, which may include family caregiving and household health routines. The result suggests a beginner topic should avoid “all family change” and focus on a narrow observable practice such as communication, caregiving, chores, religion, or technology use.  

A second search should narrow the sociological angle to family life, household routines, and intergenerational change, because “families are changing over time” is too broad for an introductory project. I’ll look for a concrete mechanism that can be studied with interviews, public statistics, or small surveys.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"family sociology household routines technology communication intergenerational change COVID online 2018 2025","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025,"k":5}
```  
[result]: The strongest recurring themes are digital communication in families, changing household roles, caregiving responsibilities, and pandemic-related shifts in home life. Among the already retrieved OpenAlex candidates, `oa:W3018139358` is the closest match because online religious/community participation can be studied as a change in family routines and social connection. The search also reinforces that a manageable beginner project should ask a “how has X changed for Y group?” question, not attempt to explain long-term transformation of the family as an institution.  

I’ll read the online church volume because it may provide a useful example of how institutions entered domestic space through digital media, which connects to changing family practices. Even if it is not a family sociology paper, it can help frame a beginner-friendly topic around household routines and online participation.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"oa:W3018139358"}
```  
[result]: `oa:W3018139358`, *The Distanced Church: Reflections on Doing Church Online* (2020), is a collection of short essays about religious communities moving online during the pandemic. It emphasizes that worship, community participation, and institutional belonging were relocated into homes through livestreams, video calls, and digital rituals. For a family-change topic, its relevance is not “family structure” directly but the way shared household practices and intergenerational participation may change when community life becomes mediated by screens.  

I’ll extract the conceptual “genome” from this paper to identify what kind of research design it implies: mechanism, niche, observations, limitations, and possible contribution. This helps decide whether it can anchor a beginner sociology question.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_id":"oa:W3018139358"}
```  
[result]: Extracted genome: mechanism = digital mediation relocates collective religious/community participation into domestic space; niche = pandemic-era online church and digitally mediated community life; observation = people adapted rituals, belonging, and participation through household screens; limitation = the work is reflective and institution-centered rather than a systematic family study; claim = online forms of community can reshape everyday practices and meanings of participation. For a beginner family project, this suggests a tractable question about how families negotiated shared or individual online participation at home.  

Next I’ll read the diabetes self-management programme because health management often involves family support, caregiving, household routines, and intergenerational obligations. It may offer a second, more concrete angle: how families adapt when care becomes digitally supported or home-managed.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"oa:W2889778582"}
```  
[result]: `oa:W2889778582`, *A web-based self-management programme for people with type 2 diabetes: the HeLP-Diabetes research programme including RCT* (2018), evaluates a web-based diabetes self-management intervention. Its primary field is health services research rather than sociology, but it highlights a broader social shift toward digital self-management and care work occurring in homes. Family members are not the core analytic focus, yet the topic is useful for thinking about how household support, responsibility, and daily routines can change around chronic illness.  

I’ll extract this second genome to compare it against the online-community angle. The goal is to find the more beginner-manageable family sociology topic: digital community at home, family caregiving, or some combination of both.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_id":"oa:W2889778582"}
```  
[result]: Extracted genome: mechanism = web-based self-management shifts some health knowledge, monitoring, and responsibility into everyday domestic routines; niche = type 2 diabetes management and digital health support; observation = digital tools can support patient behavior change but require engagement and practical integration into daily life; limitation = the unit of analysis is the patient/intervention, not family relations; claim = digital self-management can improve or support chronic illness care, but household context is underdeveloped. For a beginner sociology project, this points to family caregiving and “who helps with health routines?” as a possible narrow research question.  

Now I’ll run a novelty check on the most promising mechanism: digital mediation moving social participation and care responsibilities into the home. This will show whether the beginner topic needs to be framed modestly as a local exploratory study rather than a broad original theory claim.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","mechanism":"digital mediation shifts community participation, care, and routine coordination into family home life, changing how household members share responsibilities and maintain relationships","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025}
```  
[result]: The mechanism is not wholly novel: recent literature commonly examines digital family communication, online schooling/work, telehealth, and pandemic home life. However, a narrow beginner project can still be valuable if it focuses on a specific population, practice, or comparison, such as “college students’ contact with parents,” “grandparents and video calls,” or “families managing religious or health routines online.” The strongest gap for a beginner is not theoretical novelty but empirical manageability: choosing a small observable change and collecting limited data.  

I’ll test a proposed topic against the extracted genome from the online church paper, because it is the better sociological fit for “families changing over time” than the health intervention paper. The proposed genome will narrow the project to intergenerational digital communication and household routines, which a beginner could study through interviews.

The novelty is moderate but appropriate for a beginner because it turns a broad historical question into a small empirical project. The main caution is to avoid claiming “families have changed” in general; the project should claim only that a specific practice shows perceived change among a defined group.  

Given the searches, readings, genome extraction, novelty check, and diff, I’ll propose a beginner-friendly sociology topic that is narrow, feasible, and connected to family change over time. full_prompt: In laser-plasma interaction physics, fluid models and particle-in-cell simulations are used to study inertial confinement fusion and high-intensity laser experiments. chosen:

```action
{
  "type": "propose",
  "gene_genome": {
    "idea": "Develop an adaptive hybrid fluid–kinetic laser-plasma model in which the bulk dense plasma is evolved with radiation-hydrodynamics or two-fluid equations, while localized regions with strong non-Maxwellian behavior are dynamically upgraded to reduced PIC/Vlasov kinetic patches.",
    "mechanism": "Use local indicators such as large heat-flux deviation from Spitzer-Härm transport, steep velocity-space gradients, anomalous absorption, two-plasmon/Weibel/Raman instability thresholds, or suprathermal electron fraction to trigger kinetic subdomains near the laser critical surface, shocks, or instability fronts.",
    "implementation": "Couple the fluid solver to embedded kinetic patches through conservative exchange of charge, current, momentum, and energy; represent hot electrons leaving kinetic regions as particle packets or distribution-function moments injected into the fluid domain with flux-limited transport and self-consistent electromagnetic fields.",
    "target_prediction": "Accurately predict hot-electron energy spectra, angular distributions, absorption fraction, preheat, and instability growth rates in dense laser-plasma interaction regimes relevant to inertial confinement fusion and high-intensity laser experiments.",
    "cost_reduction": "Restrict expensive kinetic resolution to small, dynamically selected phase-space/physical-space regions while treating the majority of the dense plasma as a low-noise continuum, reducing particle noise and computational cost relative to full-domain PIC.",
    "validation_plan": "Benchmark against full PIC in reduced geometries for laser absorption and instability growth, then compare integrated hybrid simulations with experimental observables such as x-ray emission, electron spectrometers, reflected-light spectra, and target preheat diagnostics."
  }
}
```

进一步阅读相关方向摘要，确认可行的创新切入点：不是单独做延迟补偿，也不是单独做被动性注入，而是让预测控制在优化过程中主动满足能量一致性约束。

full_prompt: 总结植物开花时间调控的主要分子通路，包括光周期、春化和激素信号。请用自由文本回答。
chosen:
先检索“开花时间调控”综述与经典遗传通路，目标是把分散的基因—信号关系压缩成可比较的机制骨架。  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"plant flowering time regulation photoperiod vernalization hormone signaling FT CO FLC gibberellin review","domain":"biology","max_results":5}
```  
[result]: 检索结果集中在拟南芥模型：光周期通路以昼夜钟—GI—CO—FT为核心，春化通路以VIN3/VRN复合体介导FLC表观遗传沉默为核心，激素以赤霉素为最直接促花信号。还提示温度、年龄、糖信号与自主通路会汇入SOC1、LFY、AP1等花分生组织整合因子。  

将检索到的通路按“输入信号—核心节点—输出基因—生态位条件”抽取为基因组式摘要，便于最终给出结构化总结。  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","source":"searched pathway summaries","schema":["mechanism_genome","niche_genome","observation_genome","limitation_genome","delta_genome","claim_genome"]}
```  
[result]: 抽取结果显示：FT/TSF是叶片感光与昼夜节律信息向茎尖传递的关键移动信号；FLC是春化前抑制开花的中心阻遏因子；GA通过DELLA降解解除对SOC1/LFY等基因的抑制。不同物种中同源模块保守但功能方向可变，例如水稻Hd1在短日照促花、长日照抑花。  

```action
{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"植物开花时间调控可概括为多个环境与内源信号在FT、SOC1、LFY、AP1等整合节点上的汇合。光周期通路中，光敏色素phytochrome和隐花色素cryptochrome感知红光、远红光和蓝光，昼夜钟调控GI、FKF1与CONSTANS/CO的表达和蛋白稳定性；在适宜日长下，CO激活叶片维管束中的FT/TSF，FT蛋白作为成花素移动到茎尖，与FD形成复合体，诱导SOC1、AP1、LFY等花分生组织基因。春化通路中，长期低温诱导VIN3等因子，促进PRC2相关复合体在FLC染色质上沉积H3K27me3，使FLC被稳定沉默；由于FLC平时抑制FT和SOC1，春化解除这种抑制，从而使植物在冬季后具备开花能力。激素信号中，赤霉素GA最直接参与促花：GA促进DELLA蛋白降解，解除DELLA对SOC1、LFY和PIF等因子的抑制，尤其在非诱导性短日照条件下对拟南芥开花很重要；此外，脱落酸、细胞分裂素、乙烯、油菜素内酯和生长素也可通过胁迫、营养状态、茎尖活性或转录因子网络调节FT/SOC1/LFY，但作用更依赖物种和环境。","niche_genome":"该框架主要来自拟南芥等长日照植物，但可推广到许多作物：小麦、大麦中VRN1/VRN2/FT类基因构成春化和光周期适应模块；水稻等短日照植物中Hd1、Ehd1、Hd3a、RFT1等对应CO/FT样通路，但Hd1可在不同日长下表现相反效应。生态上，这些通路使植物把开花安排在光照长度、冬季经历、温度和资源状态最有利于繁殖的季节。","observation_genome":"典型遗传观察包括：co或ft突变体在长日照下晚花；FLC高表达导致晚花，而春化或flc功能缺失促进开花；GA缺陷或DELLA稳定化通常导致晚花，外源GA可部分恢复开花。分子观察包括FT从叶片向茎尖移动、FLC位点冷诱导H3K27me3积累、以及SOC1/LFY/AP1在花转换前后上调。","limitation_genome":"这一总结偏重模式植物拟南芥，不能简单等同于所有植物；短日照作物、木本多年生植物和多年生草本中，同源基因可能发生功能分化。激素之间还存在广泛串扰，且温度、年龄、糖/Tre6P、自主通路、microRNA156/172模块和胁迫信号也会显著改变开花时间。","delta_genome":"相较于只列举单一路径，更准确的理解是：光周期决定FT表达的日长窗口，春化通过沉默FLC改变系统是否被允许响应促花信号，激素尤其GA调节茎尖对成花转换的敏感性；三者最终在FT–FD、SOC1、LFY、AP1这一花转变核心网络上汇合。","claim_genome":"植物开花时间的主要分子逻辑是：环境光周期提供季节性日长信息，冬季低温通过春化建立表观遗传记忆，内源激素反映发育和生理状态；这些信号共同调控FT/FLC/GA-DELLA/SOC1/LFY/AP1网络，从而决定营养生长向生殖生长转换的时机。"}}
```

full_prompt:
You are a top-tier researcher in your field. Based on the following context, please generate a novel and detailed research proposal.

##Context:

###1. Related Work:
- Human Brain Project (HBP): Aimed to create a digital infrastructure for neuroscience, with platforms like SpiNNaker and NEST enabling neuronal-level simulation of local and large-scale brain networks. However, these tools are constrained by limited scalability and complexity in capturing whole-brain structure and dynamics.
- The Virtual Brain (TVB): Integrates software tools, brain atlases, datasets, and computational models for simulating brain activity, currently applied in clinical trials. While TVB supports macroscale modeling, it lacks neuron-level granularity and assimilation of individual biological data at full brain scale.
- Large-scale GPU/CPU Simulations (e.g., Modha et al., Djurfeldt et al.): Implemented brain-sized network models on HPC resources, but encounter major bottlenecks in memory access, communication bandwidth, and are not yet capable of personalized, biologically-constrained, full-scale human brain emulation.

###2. Challenge:
The core challenges in advancing large-scale brain simulation research primarily revolve around scalability, biological fidelity, and computational efficiency. First, existing simulation platforms struggle with scalability, as they cannot accommodate the vast number of neurons and synapses present in the human brain, leading to incomplete models that fail to capture critical dynamics. Second, a significant limitation is the lack of individualization; current models do not fully integrate empirical biological data, which is essential for creating personalized simulations that reflect the variability in human neuroanatomy and function. Third, the communication and memory bottlenecks inherent in high-performance computing (HPC) systems pose serious obstacles, as they hinder the ability to efficiently process and transmit the vast amounts of data generated in large-scale simulations. Finally, the absence of a robust statistical assimilation framework to infer parameters from experimental neuroimaging constrains the biological plausibility and predictive capabilities of these models, as they cannot effectively leverage real-world data to enhance accuracy and relevance. Overcoming these challenges is crucial for achieving more comprehensive and realistic representations of brain function and aiding in the understanding of neurological disorders.

###3. Limitations of Existing Approaches:
Existing approaches are either limited in scale (unable to reach human brain neuron/synapse numbers), lack individualization from empirical data, or neglect the communication and memory bottlenecks of heterogeneous, large-scale brain simulations. Moreover, most do not offer an explicit statistical assimilation framework to infer parameters from experimental neuroimaging, thereby restricting biological plausibility and predictive power.

###4. Motivation for New Research:
The human brain's complexity demands simulation at an unprecedented scale and fidelity to bridge the gap between neuroscience, computation, and personalized medicine. Realizing a digital brain that assimilates empirical data and supports hypothesis-driven 'dry experiments' is key to advancing understanding of brain dynamics, disease mechanisms, and brain-inspired artificial intelligence.

###5. Task Objective:
To construct and validate the Digital Brain (DB) platform: a scalable, neuron-level, biologically constrained simulation of the entire human brain (up to 86 billion neurons and 47.8 trillion synapses), assimilating individual human MRI data and reproducing both resting-state and task-evoked brain activity measured by BOLD-fMRI.

###6. Existing Solutions:
- SpiNNaker/NEsT: Enable scalable spiking neural network simulations but are limited by fixed hardware constraints and do not assimilate personalized neuroimaging data.
- TVB: Facilitates large-scale network modeling and clinical applications but operates at macroscale and lacks neuron-level simulation and parameter assimilation from biological data.
- Prior GPU/CPU Simulations: Achieved simulations of billions of neurons but suffer from inefficient communication, lack dynamic data-driven assimilation, and do not achieve full-scale, individualized, biophysically detailed brain models.

##Your Task:

Based on the context above, please generate the following sections for a new research proposal. Be specific, clear, and innovative. Please limit the generated idea to 500 characters.

###1. Idea:
(Based on the above information, please propose an innovative and feasible idea. Include the required professional methods, reasoning, and logical development. Please limit your proposal to 300-500 characters.)

###2. ImplementationSteps:
(Provide a complete and specific list of implementation steps for your idea, and number each step.)

###3. ImplementationOrder:
(Provide a formatted execution route and a checklist of implementation steps. For example, "1-2", "2-3", "3-4", "4-5".)

###4. Dataset:
(Describe the dataset required for the evaluation. If it needs to be created, explain how.)

###5. EvaluationMetrics:
(Define specific, measurable metrics to evaluate the success of the project. Explain why these metrics are relevant.)

###6. ExpectedOutcome:
(Describe the anticipated results and their potential impact on the field. Compare them to the existing solutions.)
chosen:
I first map the post-2018 landscape around personalized whole-brain modeling, because the proposal must go beyond TVB-style macroscale dynamics while preserving clinical data assimilation. This query targets TVB, virtual patients, and Bayesian/clinical personalization.  
```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "personalized Virtual Brain Bayesian assimilation whole brain simulation neuroimaging epilepsy 2018 2025", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025, "k": 5}
```  
[result]: The search returns TVB-centered clinical and methodological papers, including Schirner et al. on the TVB simulation ecosystem (OpenAlex: https://openalex.org/W2794404553), Hashemi et al. on Bayesian virtual epileptic patients (https://openalex.org/W3080301908), and Jirsa et al. on personalized virtual brain models in epilepsy (https://openalex.org/W4386998857). Results emphasize macroscale connectome-based models and patient-specific parameter inference, but not neuron/synapse-scale personalization.

Next I search the HPC/spiking-network side, focusing on memory/communication bottlenecks and extreme-scale spiking simulations. This angle tests whether recent simulators solve the human-scale problem or mainly demonstrate synthetic scaling.  
```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "extreme scale spiking neural network simulation NEST GPU memory communication bottleneck 2018 2025", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025, "k": 5}
```  
[result]: The search highlights Jordan et al.’s extreme-scale cortical-network simulations (https://openalex.org/W2789075181), Knight and Nowotny on GPU spiking simulations (https://openalex.org/W2909258571), and Golosio et al. on large-scale Izhikevich simulations (https://openalex.org/W2947473067). These works show strong engineering progress but generally lack individualized biological constraints and online statistical assimilation from neuroimaging.

I then search for neuromorphic and heterogeneous hardware, because the bottleneck may require event-driven, locality-aware compute rather than only larger CPU/GPU clusters. This query covers SpiNNaker/Loihi-style systems and hybrid simulation architectures.  
```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "neuromorphic manycore brain simulation SpiNNaker Loihi heterogeneous event driven 2018 2025 neuroscience", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025, "k": 5}
```  
[result]: Results include Davies et al. on Intel Loihi (https://openalex.org/W2893728844), Rhodes et al. on SpiNNaker software and large-scale spiking applications (https://openalex.org/W3014570327), and Yan et al. on brain-inspired neuromorphic architectures (https://openalex.org/W4226306867). The main finding is that neuromorphic platforms reduce energy and communication costs for event-driven workloads, but remain difficult to couple with rich individualized anatomy, multimodal neuroimaging, and probabilistic parameter inference.

I read the TVB ecosystem paper to identify the exact boundary between clinically useful macroscale models and missing neuron-level detail. This establishes the main baseline the proposal must extend rather than replace.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "https://openalex.org/W2794404553"}
```  
[result]: Schirner et al. describe TVB as a brain-network simulation framework that links structural connectivity, neural mass models, and empirical signals such as EEG/MEG/fMRI. The paper’s strength is subject-level whole-brain modeling with realistic connectivity and clinical workflows. Its limitation for this prompt is that model units are mostly region-level neural masses/fields, so cellular heterogeneity, synaptic microcircuit constraints, and full-brain spiking dynamics are abstracted away.

I read the Bayesian virtual epileptic patient work because it directly addresses statistical assimilation and patient-specific parameter inference. The proposal should preserve this inferential rigor while changing the simulation substrate.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "https://openalex.org/W3080301908"}
```  
[result]: Hashemi et al. formulate a Bayesian framework for inferring epileptogenic-zone parameters in personalized TVB models using patient data. The paper demonstrates that uncertainty-aware parameter estimation can turn a simulator into a clinically interpretable virtual patient. However, the inferred parameters are tied to reduced neural-mass models, and the assimilation loop is not designed for trillion-synapse event streams or hierarchical neuron-to-region mappings.

I read the extreme-scale spiking simulation paper to understand whether state-of-the-art HPC already solves the scaling problem. This gives the engineering baseline for memory layout, routing, and communication costs.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "https://openalex.org/W2789075181"}
```  
[result]: Jordan et al. show that cortical-scale spiking networks can be simulated on leadership-class supercomputers using careful domain decomposition and communication strategies. The paper makes clear that spike exchange, synaptic state memory, and irregular access patterns dominate performance at scale. It is not a personalized human-brain emulation framework: the network is primarily a benchmark-like cortical microcircuit expansion, not constrained by an individual’s MRI/dMRI/EEG/fMRI and not embedded in Bayesian data assimilation.

I read the Loihi paper because neuromorphic manycore processors may provide the event-locality and energy efficiency needed for spiking full-brain simulation. This helps distinguish hardware acceleration from a complete biologically constrained modeling framework.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "https://openalex.org/W2893728844"}
```  
[result]: Davies et al. present Loihi as a manycore neuromorphic processor with asynchronous spike communication, local learning, and low-power event-driven computation. Its architecture is well aligned with sparse spiking workloads and could reduce communication/energy costs relative to dense synchronous HPC. The paper does not, however, solve atlas-to-microcircuit construction, subject-specific parameter inference, or multimodal neuroimaging assimilation.

Having surveyed clinical TVB, Bayesian virtual patients, HPC spiking simulation, and neuromorphic hardware, I propose a hybrid framework rather than a single monolithic simulator. The novelty is a hierarchical personalized digital brain that uses statistical assimilation to couple individual neuroimaging to adaptive multiresolution spiking/mass models deployed on heterogeneous CPU/GPU/neuromorphic hardware.  
```action
{"tool": "propose", "gene_genome": {"mechanism_genome": "Develop a Hierarchical Assimilative Neuromorphic Digital Brain (HANDB): a multiresolution whole-brain simulator in which each subject's MRI/dMRI-derived connectome defines region-to-region scaffold connectivity; each region is represented by an adaptive mixture of neural-mass surrogates and sampled spiking microcircuits; and a Bayesian/variational assimilation layer continuously infers latent excitability, synaptic gain, conduction delay, plasticity, and noise parameters from EEG/MEG/fMRI/iEEG.
