The strongest opportunity is not another global maximum estimate, but a constraint-weighted sequestration assessment that couples rock supply, kinetic weathering, land suitability, and side-effect risk.  
```action
{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"Enhanced rock weathering spreads finely ground mafic/ultramafic silicate minerals, especially basalt or olivine-rich materials, onto soils where dissolution consumes CO2 through alkalinity generation and eventual bicarbonate/carbonate storage.","niche_genome":"Focus on croplands and managed grasslands near low-carbon quarry/mine-waste sources, prioritizing humid warm regions with high rainfall, acidic soils, and existing lime/fertilizer logistics while excluding conservation lands and water-stressed regions.","observation_genome":"Global mineral abundance is large, but practically available low-cost, low-contaminant feedstock is spatially uneven; field weathering rates are slower and more variable than lab rates, controlled by grain size, soil pH, hydrology, temperature, biota, and repeated application depth.","limitation_genome":"Key constraints are grinding and transport energy, competition for land-management operations, uncertain monitoring/reporting/verification of dissolved inorganic carbon export, heavy-metal release from some ultramafic rocks, dust, nutrient imbalance, river/ocean alkalinity perturbation, and possible N2O or soil-ecology impacts.","delta_genome":"Develop a geospatial ERW suitability model that jointly scores mineral supply chains, kinetic weathering potential, cropland compatibility, life-cycle emissions, and environmental side-effect risk rather than reporting gross theoretical CO2 removal potential.","claim_genome":"Enhanced rock weathering can plausibly contribute durable CO2 removal at regional-to-gigaton scale only where fast-weathering suitable minerals, short transport distances, receptive agricultural soils, robust MRV, and contaminant safeguards coincide; its best role is a constrained portfolio component, not a universal standalone climate solution."}}
```
rejected:
I’ll start by doing a fairly targeted scan, but I want to be careful not to overfit the proposal to just one highly cited paper or one optimistic global estimate. Enhanced rock weathering has a lot of moving parts — mineral supply, cropland deployment, dissolution kinetics, transport, grinding energy, MRV, contaminant risks, and so on — so even a quick literature anchoring should check the core ERW papers and the constraint-oriented discussions. I’ll first search broadly for ERW on croplands, mineral availability, weathering rates, and environmental impacts, because those are likely to be the core axes of the research genome.
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"enhanced rock weathering CO2 removal croplands mineral availability weathering rates environmental impacts","year_min":2016,"year_max":2024,"k":5}
```
[result]: Found core candidates including Beerling et al. 2020 Nature, OpenAlex `W3037170024`, on cropland ERW potential; Strefler et al. 2018 ERL, OpenAlex `W2741809807`, on global potential/costs; and Eufrasio et al. 2022, OpenAlex `W4287446243`, on environmental and deployment constraints.

That already gives the basic foundation: Beerling et al. is the obvious high-level cropland ERW anchor, Strefler et al. is important for global potential and costs, and Eufrasio et al. is useful because it points toward constraints instead of only theoretical maxima. Still, I want to make sure I’m not missing the recurring points around practical deployment. A lot of ERW discussions can become too optimistic if they focus only on global mineral abundance or idealized weathering rates, so I should repeat a similar query with slightly different wording to see whether the same constraint literature comes up again.
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"enhanced weathering cropland basalt olivine carbon dioxide removal constraints mineral supply environmental risks","year_min":2016,"year_max":2024,"k":5}
```
[result]: Returned overlapping ERW literature emphasizing basalt and olivine applications, cropland deployment, kinetic uncertainty, energy requirements for comminution, transport-distance sensitivity, and environmental concerns including trace metals and soil/water impacts.

This is basically confirming the same shape of the literature. The opportunity is probably not “ERW exists and could remove CO2,” because that is already well covered. It is also probably not just “calculate global maximum potential,” because those estimates are highly sensitive to assumptions about feedstock, grinding, spreading, weathering kinetics, and agricultural land availability. The stronger angle is more conditional: where exactly do all the necessary conditions coincide? Suitable minerals, short transport, receptive soils, adequate rainfall and temperature, low life-cycle emissions, low contaminant risk, and plausible MRV all need to line up.

I’ll do one more near-duplicate search, partly to make sure environmental side effects and monitoring/reporting/verification are represented, since those are easy to understate if the proposal is framed too much around geochemical potential alone.
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"enhanced rock weathering MRV dissolved inorganic carbon heavy metals soil impacts croplands basalt","year_min":2016,"year_max":2024,"k":5}
```
[result]: Found additional overlapping discussions on ERW monitoring, dissolved inorganic carbon export, soil chemistry changes, trace-metal release risks from some rock types, and the need to distinguish laboratory dissolution rates from field-scale weathering.

So the repeated scans keep pointing to the same bottleneck: ERW is promising, but practical estimates need to be constraint-weighted. It is not enough to know that basalt or ultramafic minerals can weather and consume CO2. One needs to ask whether the rock is actually available at appropriate purity and cost, whether it can be ground finely without erasing too much of the carbon benefit, whether transport distances are short, whether soils and climates support sufficiently fast dissolution, whether farmers can actually incorporate this into existing management, and whether adverse effects like metal release, dust, nutrient imbalance, hydrologic export uncertainty, or N2O interactions are manageable.

In other words, the core proposal should not be framed as a new global maximum estimate or another headline gigaton number. Those numbers can be useful, but they can also hide the spatial heterogeneity that determines whether ERW works in practice. A more useful research genome is a geospatial suitability model that couples the supply-chain side and the biogeochemical side, while also explicitly scoring side-effect risk and MRV feasibility. This makes the claim more cautious but also more scientifically durable: ERW may be important, but only as a constrained portfolio component where the practical conditions align.

The strongest opportunity is not another global maximum estimate, but a constraint-weighted sequestration assessment that couples rock supply, kinetic weathering, land suitability, and side-effect risk.

```action
{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"奖励预期由试次开始的线索诱发：例如金币图标提示高奖励，灰色图标提示低奖励。随后被试执行一个中央主任务，如判断数字奇偶或字母大小；与此同时，屏幕旁侧短暂呈现任务无关刺激，如面孔、物体词、伪词或彩色图形。关键假设是，高奖励预期会提高整体任务投入和增益控制，但这种增益可能外溢到任务无关刺激，使其获得更深的知觉、身份或语义加工。","niche_genome":"现有研究多关注奖励关联刺激如何捕获注意，或者奖励如何改善目标加工。本研究聚焦一个更细的问题：当奖励只与主任务表现有关、旁侧刺激完全无关且不预测奖励时，奖励预期是否仍会改变这些无关刺激的加工深度。","observation_genome":"实验流程可分为四个阶段。第一，呈现奖励线索，提示本试次正确且快速反应可获得高奖励或低奖励。第二，出现中央任务刺激，同时或稍后出现旁侧任务无关刺激；旁侧刺激可分为低层视觉变化、可识别物体、情绪面孔或有语义意义的词。第三，被试只回应中央任务。第四，少数探测试次要求被试意外识别或记忆刚才的旁侧刺激，以评估无关刺激是否被深层编码。行为指标包括中央任务反应时、正确率、旁侧刺激的事后识别率、语义启动效应和干扰效应。","limitation_genome":"主要限制是奖励预期可能同时改变唤醒、动机和注意范围，因此需要控制瞳孔大小、心率或主观动机评分。另一个限制是，如果高奖励导致更强的目标聚焦，也可能反而抑制无关刺激加工；因此实验应比较不同刺激出现时间和空间距离，以区分外溢增益与目标保护机制。","delta_genome":"相对于价值驱动注意捕获研究，本设计的增量在于把‘刺激曾经被奖励’与‘当前处于奖励预期状态’分开，并用多层神经指标区分加工深度。可能的神经指标包括：早期视觉ERP成分P1/N1反映感觉增益；N2pc或Pd反映无关刺激是否被选择或被主动抑制；N400反映词语或物体的语义加工；P3/LPP反映更深层的身份更新或情绪评价；若使用fMRI，可检测视觉皮层、梭状回、腹侧颞叶和前额叶-纹状体奖励网络的活动，并用MVPA测试无关刺激类别是否可被解码。","claim_genome":"如果高奖励预期增强任务无关刺激的P1/N1但不增强N400或记忆表现，说明奖励主要提高浅层感觉增益；如果同时增强N400、P3、后续识别率或fMRI类别解码，则说明奖励预期会使无关刺激进入更深的身份/语义加工。若高奖励降低旁侧刺激解码或诱发更强Pd，则支持相反观点：奖励预期强化目标保护，从而抑制无关信息。该研究可解释奖励如何在认知系统中改变无关信息的过滤阈值。"}}
```

full_prompt: Might temperature changes alter the balance between growth and reproduction in small aquatic organisms? The rough idea is that warming shifts energy allocation, but it is unclear whether the effect depends on food availability, life stage, or population density.
chosen:
I start with a broad search for recent aquatic ectotherm life-history studies linking temperature, food, and allocation, so the first hypothesis is anchored in the 2018–2025 literature rather than only in metabolic intuition.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"2018 2025 temperature food availability growth reproduction allocation Daphnia rotifer copepod aquatic ectotherm life history","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025,"k":5}
```

[result]: The search returns several relevant recent studies on zooplankton and other small aquatic ectotherms, especially Daphnia and rotifers. The most directly relevant OpenAlex records include `W4287890320` on warming-food interactions in Daphnia life histories, `W4387078363` on temperature-dependent resource limitation in planktonic consumers, and `W4312881845` on thermal effects on reproductive timing and body size. These suggest that temperature effects are rarely purely additive and often depend on resource supply and developmental stage.

Given that background, I formulate a rough first-pass hypothesis: warming changes the allocation balance by raising metabolic demand and accelerating development, but the direction of the growth–reproduction tradeoff depends on food availability.

```action
{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"Temperature increase raises metabolic maintenance costs and developmental rate in small aquatic ectotherms, forcing reallocation between somatic growth and reproductive output.","niche_genome":"Small aquatic organisms such as Daphnia, rotifers, and copepod nauplii exposed to experimentally elevated temperatures across food gradients.","observation_genome":"Measure body growth, size at maturity, age at first reproduction, clutch size, egg production rate, and survival under crossed temperature and food treatments.","limitation_genome":"Initial version treats density and life stage as secondary covariates and does not specify whether warming favors earlier reproduction, reduced growth, or both.","delta_genome":"Moves beyond a simple temperature-size response by explicitly framing warming as an allocation tradeoff between growth and reproduction.","claim_genome":"Warming will shift energy allocation away from somatic growth toward reproduction when food is sufficient, but will suppress both growth and reproduction under food limitation."}}
```

[result]: The rough proposal is coherent but still underspecified. It captures temperature × food dependence, yet it does not adequately distinguish juvenile growth, maturation, and adult reproduction, and it ignores population density as a mechanism producing food limitation or interference. The next step is to compare this proposal against a close parent paper to see what is missing.

I compare the rough genome against a representative recent Daphnia temperature–resource study to identify gaps in mechanism and experimental design.

```action
{"tool":"genome_diff","parent_id":"W4287890320","proposed_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"Temperature increase raises metabolic maintenance costs and developmental rate in small aquatic ectotherms, forcing reallocation between somatic growth and reproductive output.","niche_genome":"Small aquatic organisms such as Daphnia, rotifers, and copepod nauplii exposed to experimentally elevated temperatures across food gradients.","observation_genome":"Measure body growth, size at maturity, age at first reproduction, clutch size, egg production rate, and survival under crossed temperature and food treatments.","limitation_genome":"Initial version treats density and life stage as secondary covariates and does not specify whether warming favors earlier reproduction, reduced growth, or both.","delta_genome":"Moves beyond a simple temperature-size response by explicitly framing warming as an allocation tradeoff between growth and reproduction.","claim_genome":"Warming will shift energy allocation away from somatic growth toward reproduction when food is sufficient, but will suppress both growth and reproduction under food limitation."}}
```

[result]: The genome differs from the parent by emphasizing explicit allocation tradeoffs rather than only performance curves, but the diff flags three missing components. First, density is not merely a covariate because it changes per-capita food supply and chemical/interference stress. Second, life stage matters because juveniles allocate primarily to growth while mature adults allocate to eggs. Third, the proposal needs a measurable mediator, such as “scope for growth,” lipid reserves, RNA:DNA ratio, or egg-to-body-mass allocation.

I then check whether the basic mechanism is novel enough, or whether the literature has already established this exact temperature–food allocation interaction.

```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","mechanism":"warming alters growth versus reproduction allocation in small aquatic ectotherms depending on food availability, life stage, and density-mediated resource limitation","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025}
```

[result]: The novelty check finds many studies on temperature-size rules, thermal performance, and food-dependent fecundity, so the broad idea is not novel. However, fewer papers jointly test temperature × food × density while separating juvenile growth allocation from adult reproductive allocation. The most promising refinement is to predict a stage-specific allocation switch, with density acting as an ecological amplifier of food limitation.

To fill the missing pieces, I search specifically for studies combining density, life stage, and resource effects in small planktonic organisms under warming.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"Daphnia rotifer temperature density food life stage growth reproduction lipid allocation 2018 2025","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025,"k":5}
```

[result]: The search highlights work on density dependence in zooplankton thermal responses, including `W4387078363`, `W4312881845`, and `W4391122457`. Several results emphasize that crowding lowers per-capita food intake and can change maturation timing, body size, and clutch production. One particularly useful record, `W4387078363`, appears to connect warming with resource limitation and consumer energy budgets.

I read the most useful paper to extract design details and mediators that can refine the hypothesis.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"W4387078363"}
```

[result]: The paper reports that warming increases metabolic demand and can intensify resource limitation when food is finite or when consumer density is high. It distinguishes juvenile somatic growth from adult reproductive output and recommends tracking body carbon, lipid reserves, egg production, and per-capita ingestion rather than only final body size. It also notes that apparent “warming benefits” at low density can reverse at high density because elevated metabolism depletes reserves faster.

Using those refinements, I revise the hypothesis into a more testable, stage-aware, density-aware allocation model.

```action
{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"Warming increases metabolic maintenance costs and accelerates development, raising the minimum food intake needed to maintain positive scope for growth. In juveniles, energy is preferentially allocated to somatic growth until a maturation threshold is reached; in adults, allocation shifts toward eggs only when food intake exceeds maintenance plus reserve-rebuilding costs. High population density lowers per-capita food availability and may add crowding stress, so it converts warming from a reproduction-accelerating factor into an allocation constraint that reduces both body growth and fecundity.","niche_genome":"Small aquatic ectotherms with short generation times, especially Daphnia, rotifers, and copepod early stages, in freshwater or plankton microcosms exposed to factorial combinations of temperature, food concentration, and population density. The most relevant range is nonlethal warming within ecologically realistic temperatures, where organisms survive but energy budgets are altered.","observation_genome":"Run a factorial experiment crossing temperature, algal food level, density, and life stage or age cohort. Track juvenile growth rate, size at maturity, age at first reproduction, adult body size, clutch size, egg production rate, inter-clutch interval, survival, ingestion rate, lipid reserves, RNA:DNA or carbon:nitrogen proxies, and egg mass relative to maternal body mass. The critical observation is whether warming increases early reproduction only under high food and low density, but decreases growth and fecundity under low food or high density.","limitation_genome":"The hypothesis is strongest for short-term plastic responses and may not predict evolutionary adaptation, maternal effects across many generations, predator-induced life-history shifts, disease, or community-level food-web feedbacks. It also assumes temperature remains below acute thermal stress thresholds; near lethal limits, direct physiological damage may dominate allocation tradeoffs.","delta_genome":"Refines the simple claim that warming shifts energy from growth to reproduction by adding a conditional, stage-specific allocation switch. The new contribution is that food availability and density determine whether warming produces earlier reproduction, reduced body size, reproductive collapse, or simultaneous suppression of growth and reproduction.","claim_genome":"Temperature-driven allocation responses in small aquatic organisms are conditional rather than universal: under high food and low density, moderate warming should accelerate maturation and shift adults toward earlier or greater reproductive investment at the cost of smaller adult size; under low food or high density, warming should reduce scope for growth, lower lipid reserves, delay or reduce reproduction, and suppress juvenile growth. Therefore, the apparent balance between growth and reproduction will depend jointly on food supply, life stage, and population density."}}
```

[result]: Final refined hypothesis generated. It now specifies mechanism, organismal niche, measurable predictions, limitations, and the conceptual advance over a simple warming-allocation idea.

full_prompt: Design a learning algorithm based on synchronization phenomena in coupled oscillator systems for clustering or representation learning. How could phase locking reveal latent structure in complex datasets?
chosen:
Rationale: First search the physics literature for synchronization mechanisms that could be borrowed as an algorithmic primitive.

```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "coupled oscillator synchronization phase locking Kuramoto model community structure cluster detection physics"}
```

[result]  
Key physics concepts found:
- Kuramoto model: oscillators with natural frequencies synchronize when coupling exceeds a critical threshold.
- Phase locking: stable phase differences emerge between coupled oscillators.
- Partial synchronization / chimera states: subsets synchronize internally while remaining desynchronized from others.
- Synchronization transitions reveal coupling structure and modularity in networks.
- Order parameters quantify global or local coherence.

---

Rationale: Now search machine-learning literature for clustering and representation-learning methods that use oscillator dynamics or graph diffusion, to identify the relevant ML problem space.

```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "oscillator neural networks clustering representation learning phase synchronization graph clustering machine learning"}
```

[result]  
Relevant ML literature found:
- Oscillatory neural networks have been used for associative memory and graph partitioning.
- Spectral clustering and diffusion maps use graph Laplacian eigenstructure to reveal latent clusters.
- Contrastive learning and graph neural networks learn embeddings by preserving neighborhood structure.
- Existing oscillator clustering methods often rely on hand-designed coupling graphs or simple pairwise similarity.

full_prompt: 围绕家庭金融素养对资产配置多元化的影响，构建一个经济金融领域的实证研究设想，并指出可能的机制渠道与外部有效性问题。
chosen:
idea_plan

已有文献表明金融素养会影响家庭是否进入股票市场，但“资产配置多元化”可能不仅体现在持股与否，还体现在风险资产、保险、养老金、基金、债券等组合广度上。因此先检索相关研究以定位经验缺口。  
```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "household financial literacy portfolio diversification asset allocation empirical study", "discipline": "economics_finance", "limit": 5}
```
[result]: 检索到核心文献集中在金融素养与股票市场参与、退休规划、风险资产持有之间的关系。较少研究直接考察家庭金融素养如何影响“资产类别多元化指数”，尤其缺少对机制渠道和外部有效性的系统讨论。

为避免与既有“金融素养—股票参与”研究重复，进一步检查将因变量扩展为家庭资产组合多元化、并纳入机制检验的创新性。  
```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "query": "financial literacy household asset allocation diversification mechanisms external validity", "candidate_papers": ["van Rooij Lusardi Alessie 2011 Financial literacy and stock market participation", "Lusardi Mitchell 2014 The Economic Importance of Financial Literacy"]}
```
[result]: 该设想与经典文献相关，但创新点在于：一是使用资产配置多元化而非单一股票参与作为结果变量；二是区分风险认知、信息获取、金融咨询使用、风险偏好四类机制；三是讨论城乡、年龄、收入和金融市场发展水平差异带来的外部有效性限制。

现将研究问题转化为可执行的实证设计，重点包括指标构造、识别策略、机制检验与异质性分析。


full_prompt: Assess the impact of parking minimum reforms on development costs, car ownership, street congestion, and housing affordability. Focus on cities that have reduced or eliminated parking requirements in dense neighborhoods.
chosen:
To anchor the trajectory, I first look for empirical studies on parking minimum reform and adjacent evidence on how parking supply affects housing production and vehicle ownership in dense urban contexts.  
```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "parking minimum reform housing development costs car ownership congestion dense neighborhoods empirical", "year_min": 2010, "year_max": 2025, "k": 5}
```  
[result]: Candidate papers include Zhan Guo on residential parking supply and car ownership in New York City (OpenAlex: W2097161175), Michael Manville on Los Angeles adaptive reuse parking requirements and housing development (OpenAlex: W2140818403), and C. J. Gabbe & Gregory Pierce on bundled parking costs and rents (OpenAlex: W2517782495). The strongest fit for reform impact is Manville’s Los Angeles study; Guo provides mechanism evidence on parking supply and ownership.

I read the Los Angeles reform paper because it directly examines reduced/eliminated parking requirements in dense neighborhoods through the adaptive reuse ordinance.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "W2140818403"}
```  
[result]: Manville finds that Los Angeles’s adaptive reuse ordinance, which relaxed parking requirements for older downtown buildings, enabled many residential conversions that would otherwise have been infeasible. Developers often supplied less parking than conventional minimums required, lowering project costs and supporting housing production, though the paper is less direct on post-occupancy car ownership and curb congestion.

Next I read a complementary paper on whether required/provided residential parking changes car ownership, since the topic asks about behavioral and congestion channels beyond development feasibility.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "W2097161175"}
```  
[result]: Guo’s New York City study shows that off-street residential parking supply is positively associated with household vehicle ownership even in transit-rich neighborhoods, suggesting parking reform may reduce car ownership by reducing bundled supply. It also warns that spillover parking and curb management matter: lower off-street supply does not automatically reduce street congestion unless curb space is priced or regulated.

I extract the conceptual “genome” of the Los Angeles paper to identify what is already established and where a new research contribution could add value.  
```action
{"tool": "extract_genome", "paper_id": "W2140818403"}
```  
[result]: Extracted genome: mechanism = parking minimum relief lowers fixed development costs and converts infeasible adaptive reuse projects into feasible housing; niche = downtown Los Angeles adaptive reuse ordinance; observation = developers provided fewer spaces than prior code required and produced substantial housing; limitation = limited causal comparison, limited resident-level vehicle ownership or curb-congestion outcomes; claim = minimum parking requirements can suppress dense-neighborhood housing supply.

I check whether newer work has already integrated development costs, car ownership, curb congestion, and affordability in one quasi-experimental assessment of abolished parking minimums.  
```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "mechanism": "parking minimum abolition affects housing production costs, parking supply, car ownership, curb congestion, and rents using parcel/resident/curb data in dense neighborhoods", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025}
```  
[result]: Recent studies and policy reports examine Minneapolis, Buffalo, Seattle, San Francisco, and Portland reforms, often documenting changes in parking ratios or developer choices. However, few peer-reviewed studies jointly estimate cost pass-through, car ownership adaptation, curb occupancy/congestion, and affordability outcomes with comparable treated and control neighborhoods.

The resulting proposal extends existing reform studies by combining permit/building-cost data, household vehicle registration, curb occupancy, and rent/sales outcomes across cities that reduced or eliminated minimums in dense neighborhoods.  
```action
{"tool": "propose", "gene_genome": {"mechanism_genome": "Eliminating or sharply reducing parking minimums lowers per-unit fixed development costs by allowing developers to build fewer structured or underground spaces; the lower bundled parking supply can reduce household car ownership in transit-rich dense neighborhoods, but curb congestion effects depend on whether cities actively price or manage street parking.", "niche_genome": "Dense neighborhoods in U.S. cities that removed or reduced minimum parking requirements, such as downtown Los Angeles adaptive reuse areas, Seattle urban centers, Buffalo citywide reform with dense-neighborhood subsamples, Minneapolis transit-rich districts, and San Francisco neighborhoods near transit.", "observation_genome": "Link parcel-level permits and pro formas or construction-cost proxies to delivered parking ratios, housing unit counts, rents/sales prices, vehicle-registration records, curb occupancy, parking citations, and traffic speed data before and after reform; compare treated dense neighborhoods with matched neighborhoods where requirements remained binding or changed later.", "limitation_genome": "Observed reforms are often bundled with zoning, transit, and market-cycle changes; developer savings may be absorbed by land prices rather than tenants; curb congestion is hard to separate from ride-hailing, delivery activity, and general traffic growth; and vehicle ownership responses may lag building completion.", "delta_genome": "Move beyond single-outcome studies by estimating the full reform chain: parking requirement change -> parking spaces supplied -> development cost and housing production -> rent/affordability outcomes -> resident car ownership -> curb occupancy and street congestion, with heterogeneity by transit access and curb-pricing regime.", "claim_genome": "Parking minimum reform in dense neighborhoods should reduce development costs and increase housing feasibility most reliably, modestly improve affordability where land-market pass-through is constrained or affordable-housing rules capture savings, reduce car ownership when parking is unbundled and transit access is high, and avoid worsening street congestion only where curb parking is actively managed."}}
```

full_prompt: 请分析货币政策不确定性对企业资本投资的影响机制，并结合可识别的实证策略说明如何缓解内生性问题。请仅以自由文本回答格式回答。
chosen:
我先检索货币政策不确定性、企业投资与实物期权/融资约束机制的核心文献，以便把机制拆成可检验的渠道。  
```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "monetary policy uncertainty corporate investment real options financing constraints empirical identification"}
```
[result]: 检索结果显示，相关研究主要沿三条线索展开：不确定性提高企业等待价值、抑制不可逆投资；货币政策不确定性通过融资成本和信贷供给影响资本开支；企业异质性集中在融资约束、利率敏感度和资产可抵押性上。

接着抽取可用于识别的“研究设计基因”：冲击来源、处理组差异、结果变量与内生性威胁。  
```action
{"tool": "extract_genome", "papers": ["monetary policy uncertainty and firm investment", "uncertainty shocks and corporate investment", "monetary policy surprises and firm financing"]}
```
[result]: 可识别策略包括：使用高频货币政策意外或MPU指数的外生变动作为冲击；按企业事前利率暴露、债务期限结构、融资约束程度构造差异化处理强度；在企业固定效应、行业-时间固定效应和事件窗口设计中缓解遗漏变量与反向因果问题。

最后形成一个简短研究提案，明确机制、实证设定和内生性缓解方案。  
```action
{"tool": "propose", "answer": "本研究拟分析货币政策不确定性如何影响企业资本投资。核心机制是：当未来政策利率路径、流动性条件和融资成本更难预测时，企业面对不可逆投资会提高等待价值，从而推迟资本开支；同时，银行和债券市场可能提高风险溢价，使外部融资依赖较高的企业投资下降更明显。\n\n实证上，可构造企业层面的资本投资率作为被解释变量，以货币政策不确定性指数或高频货币政策意外作为主要解释变量，并与企业事前融资约束、短期债务占比、利率敏感负债占比等变量交互。若货币政策不确定性主要通过融资渠道发挥作用，则高融资约束、短债压力更大或外部融资依赖更强的企业，其投资下降应更显著。\n\n为缓解内生性问题，研究设计可采用企业固定效应控制不随时间变化的企业特质，采用行业-年份或地区-年份固定效应吸收共同需求冲击，并使用滞后一期企业特征来划分处理强度，避免企业当期投资决策反向影响分组变量。进一步，可利用货币政策会议前后高频金融市场意外作为准外生冲击，在窄事件窗口内识别政策不确定性的变化对企业后续投资的影响。\n\n此外，可进行若干稳健性检验：替换投资指标，如资本支出率、固定资产增长率；区分国有与民营企业、制造业与非制造业；加入现金流、托宾Q、杠杆率等控制变量；并检验现金持有、债务融资成本和银行贷款可得性是否作为中介变量。若结果显示不确定性上升显著抑制投资，且效应集中于融资约束企业，则支持“实物期权等待效应+融资约束放大效应”的解释。"}
```

```action
{"tool": "propose", "answer": "本研究拟探讨城市公园建设如何提升居民心理健康与社区互动。核心机制是：更多公共绿地能够改善居民日常休闲环境，增加户外活动频率，并通过邻里交流、运动参与和环境舒适度提升居民幸福感。\n\n实证上，可选取不同城市或社区的绿地覆盖率、公园可达性和步行距离作为主要解释变量，以居民心理健康评分、生活满意度、社交频率和运动时长作为被解释变量。研究可比较公园新建前后周边居民的变化，并进一步区分老年人、儿童家庭和通勤压力较高群体的异质性影响。\n\n为缓解内生性问题，可控制社区收入水平、人口密度、住房价格、公共服务供给和交通便利程度等因素，同时加入社区固定效应和年份固定效应。若数据允许，可利用城市规划中的公园建设批次或绿化更新项目作为外生变化来源，考察公共空间改善对居民健康与社会资本的影响。\n\n此外，可进行稳健性检验：替换绿地指标，如人均绿地面积、最近公园距离、绿道长度；替换结果变量，如焦虑程度、睡眠质量、社区信任度；并检验空气质量、噪音下降和体育活动是否发挥中介作用。若结果显示公园可达性提升显著改善居民心理健康并增强社区互动，则说明城市公共空间建设具有重要的社会福利价值。"}
```

Now I’ll propose a focused research design that compares urban and rural energy poverty through income, housing quality, prices, and clean-technology access.  
```action
{"tool": "propose", "answer": "This project will investigate energy poverty as a place-based and distributional problem affecting both urban and rural households. Building on environmental justice perspectives, it will examine how low income, poor housing quality, volatile energy prices, and unequal access to clean technologies combine to produce high household energy burdens. The central research question is: how do the drivers of energy poverty differ between urban and rural communities, and which policy interventions most effectively reduce household energy burdens in each setting?\n\nThe study will use a mixed-methods comparative design. Quantitatively, it will construct household energy burden measures, such as the share of income spent on electricity, heating, cooking fuel, and transport energy where relevant. These measures will be linked to household income, dwelling characteristics, local energy prices, heating and cooling needs, and access to technologies such as efficient appliances, rooftop solar, heat pumps, clean cooking systems, and weatherization programs. The analysis will compare urban neighborhoods, where rental housing, heat islands, and utility shutoffs may be central, with rural communities, where older housing stock, longer travel distances, weaker grid infrastructure, and limited clean-technology markets may be more important.\n\nQualitatively, the project will conduct interviews or focus groups with households, local energy providers, housing agencies, and community organizations. These interviews will identify lived experiences that are often missed in expenditure data, including tradeoffs between energy bills, food, medicine, rent, and mobility. They will also explore barriers to clean-technology adoption, such as landlord-tenant split incentives, lack of credit, upfront costs, low contractor availability, distrust of programs, and inadequate information.\n\nThe expected contribution is a typology of urban and rural energy poverty pathways. For urban communities, the project may identify affordability, rental housing quality, cooling demand, and shutoff risk as dominant mechanisms. For rural communities, it may find that inefficient homes, delivered fuels, grid reliability, transport costs, and lack of access to clean-energy programs play larger roles. The final output will be a policy framework matching interventions to community conditions, including targeted bill assistance, weatherization, tenant protections, community solar, clean heating and cooling subsidies, rural grid upgrades, and financing models that reduce upfront costs for low-income households."}
```

full_prompt: 讨论睡眠剥夺对前额叶皮层执行功能和突触稳态的影响。请使用自由文本，结合动物实验与人类成像证据。
chosen:
我先用一次检索把动物突触证据与人类前额叶成像证据对齐，重点找“执行功能—前额叶网络—突触稳态”三者之间的可检验桥梁。  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"sleep deprivation prefrontal cortex executive function synaptic homeostasis animal study human fMRI review","k":5}
```
[result]: 检索结果显示：动物研究普遍支持睡眠不足会扰乱前额叶皮层突触可塑性、树突棘周转和突触强度再归一化；人类 fMRI/EEG 研究则反复观察到睡眠剥夺后背外侧前额叶、前扣带和顶叶控制网络活动下降或代偿性增强。主要空白在于：动物的突触稳态指标与人类执行功能成像指标仍缺少跨尺度、同构任务下的直接整合。  

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"本研究拟讨论并检验睡眠剥夺如何通过破坏前额叶皮层的突触稳态，进而损害执行功能。核心假设是：清醒时间延长会导致前额叶突触强度、树突棘活动和兴奋/抑制平衡持续偏移，而正常睡眠本应完成的突触下调与网络再校准不足，最终表现为工作记忆、反应抑制、认知灵活性和情绪调控下降。\n\n动物实验部分可采用小鼠急性睡眠剥夺与恢复睡眠范式，聚焦内侧前额叶皮层和眶额/前扣带同源区域。通过双光子成像、突触蛋白定量、膜片钳记录和单细胞转录组，观察睡眠剥夺后树突棘密度、AMPA/NMDA 受体比例、微小兴奋性突触后电流以及抑制性中间神经元功能是否发生异常。若恢复睡眠能够部分逆转突触强度累积或棘突不稳定，则可支持“睡眠维持前额叶突触稳态”的机制链条。\n\n人类证据部分可结合功能磁共振、结构 MRI、EEG 慢波活动和行为任务。被试在正常睡眠、24 小时睡眠剥夺和恢复睡眠后完成 n-back、Stroop、Go/No-Go 或任务切换范式，比较背外侧前额叶、前扣带皮层与顶叶控制网络的激活和功能连接。预期睡眠剥夺会降低执行控制网络效率，增加反应时波动和错误率，并可能出现局部前额叶代偿性过度激活；EEG 慢波反弹程度则可作为突触负荷和恢复需求的非侵入性指标。\n\n该研究的意义在于把动物层面的突触稳态假说与人类层面的前额叶执行网络模型连接起来。若两类证据 convergent 地表明睡眠剥夺导致前额叶突触再归一化不足、网络信噪比下降和执行功能受损，则可为轮班工作、青少年睡眠不足、精神疾病中的认知控制障碍提供机制解释，并提示恢复睡眠、昼夜节律干预或靶向前额叶兴奋/抑制平衡的治疗策略。"} 
```

full_prompt: Study the dynamics of stellar streams in the Milky Way halo to constrain the Galaxy's gravitational potential. Evaluate whether stream perturbations reveal dark matter clumps.
chosen:
I’ll first anchor the proposal in recent stream-dynamics work focused on Milky Way potential inference and subhalo perturbations.

```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "Milky Way stellar streams gravitational potential dark matter subhalo perturbations GD-1 Pal 5", "year_min": 2015, "year_max": 2025, "k": 5}
```

[result]: Found key stream studies including Bonaca et al. on GD-1 gaps/spur interpreted as a dark-subhalo encounter, Bovy et al. on stream constraints on the Galactic potential, and Erkal et al. on Pal 5 perturbations. The literature suggests streams constrain the smooth halo potential well, but dark-clump claims remain degenerate with baryonic perturbers and progenitor effects.

I’ll now propose a compact research genome that combines smooth-potential inference with a controlled perturbation test for dark matter clumps.

```action
{"tool": "propose", "gene_genome": {"mechanism_genome": "Forward-model stellar streams as phase-space tracers in a flexible Milky Way potential, then inject localized perturbers to test whether observed density gaps, track offsets, velocity kicks, and spur-like features require dark matter subhalos beyond baryonic encounters.", "niche_genome": "Gaia-era Milky Way halo streams, especially dynamically cold streams such as GD-1, Palomar 5, and Orphan-Chenab, where precise 6D phase-space data can jointly constrain the global Galactic potential and small-scale perturbations.", "observation_genome": "Use Gaia proper motions, radial velocities, photometric distances, stream tracks, density variations, and where available chemical tagging to compare unperturbed stream models against models including encounters with subhalos, globular clusters, molecular clouds, the bar, and the Large Magellanic Cloud.", "limitation_genome": "Stream perturbations are not uniquely diagnostic of dark matter clumps because similar signatures can arise from baryonic perturbers, progenitor disruption history, observational selection, distance errors, and time-dependent effects from the LMC and Galactic bar.", "delta_genome": "Rather than treating stream gaps as standalone subhalo evidence, perform hierarchical inference across multiple streams with a shared Milky Way potential and explicit nuisance populations for baryonic perturbers, allowing the dark-subhalo mass function to be constrained only by perturbations that survive these controls.", "claim_genome": "Stellar streams can robustly improve constraints on the Milky Way gravitational potential, while evidence for dark matter clumps should be considered persuasive only when multi-stream, multi-observable perturbation patterns cannot be reproduced by baryonic perturbers or smooth time-dependent Galactic dynamics."}}
```
rejected:
I’ll propose a general research genome for using stellar streams to study the Milky Way potential and possible dark matter perturbations.

```action
{"tool": "propose", "gene_genome": {"mechanism_genome": "Model stellar streams as tracers of the Galactic gravitational field and compare their observed shapes, motions, and density structures against simulations with and without additional small-scale perturbers.", "niche_genome": "Milky Way stellar streams with available astrometric and spectroscopic measurements, especially cold streams that may preserve signatures of gravitational interactions.", "observation_genome": "Use stellar positions, proper motions, radial velocities, distances, stream tracks, and density variations to evaluate whether stream morphology is better explained by a smooth Galactic potential or by additional perturbing objects.", "limitation_genome": "Interpreting stream disturbances is difficult because gaps, offsets, or velocity features can arise from many sources, including baryonic structures, observational errors, uncertain progenitor properties, and time-dependent Galactic dynamics.", "delta_genome": "Develop a joint modeling framework that fits multiple streams simultaneously while allowing for both smooth-potential variation and possible localized perturbations, aiming to separate global gravitational effects from small-scale encounters.", "claim_genome": "Stellar streams are promising probes of the Milky Way gravitational potential, but claims about dark matter clumps require careful comparison against alternative astrophysical explanations."}}
```

full_prompt: Analyze the relationship between neighborhood gentrification and long-term residents’ sense of community. How do housing costs, local businesses, and public spaces influence social attachment?
chosen:
I’ll first locate recent sociology work connecting gentrification, displacement pressures, and neighborhood social attachment, prioritizing mechanisms around housing costs, commercial change, and shared spaces.

```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "gentrification long-term residents sense of community housing costs local businesses public spaces social attachment sociology", "year_min": 2000, "year_max": 2024, "k": 5}
```

[result]: Candidate works include Freeman 2005 on mobility in gentrifying neighborhoods, Davidson 2008 on displacement and ontological security, Brown-Saracino 2009 on neighborhood authenticity, and studies on retail change and public-space exclusion. The strongest fit centers on indirect displacement: residents may remain physically in place while losing affordability, familiar institutions, and everyday interaction sites.

I’ll read the most directly relevant candidate on gentrification, displacement, and residents’ attachment to place.

```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "https://openalex.org/W2140839671"}
```

[result]: The paper emphasizes that gentrification affects more than residential moves: rising costs, tenure insecurity, and demographic turnover reshape residents’ perceived belonging. It supports treating “sense of community” as produced through material stability, recognizable local institutions, and routine encounters in neighborhood spaces.

I propose a compact sociological genome specifying the causal pathway from gentrification to weakened or transformed community attachment.

```action
{"tool": "propose", "gene_genome": {"mechanism_genome": "Neighborhood gentrification reshapes long-term residents’ sense of community through a three-part mechanism: rising housing costs generate insecurity and anticipatory displacement; changing local businesses reduce familiar social anchors; and redesigned or privatized public spaces alter everyday interaction routines.", "niche_genome": "Urban sociology of gentrifying neighborhoods, especially long-term renters, working-class residents, elderly residents, and racialized communities who remain in place but experience social and symbolic displacement.", "observation_genome": "Even without immediate residential displacement, long-term residents may report declining belonging when rent burdens rise, legacy shops close, informal meeting places disappear, and parks, sidewalks, cafes, or plazas become oriented toward newer, wealthier users.", "limitation_genome": "The relationship is not uniformly negative: some residents may value improved services, safer public spaces, or new amenities, and effects vary by tenure, race, age, income, length of residence, and strength of preexisting neighborhood networks.", "delta_genome": "Move beyond a simple displacement-versus-revitalization frame by measuring social attachment as an interaction between material affordability, institutional continuity, and access to recognizable public life.", "claim_genome": "Gentrification weakens long-term residents’ sense of community most strongly when housing-cost pressure coincides with loss of familiar businesses and exclusion from public spaces; where affordability protections and inclusive shared spaces persist, social attachment is more likely to endure or adapt."}}
```
rejected:
I’ll propose a general sociological framing for how neighborhood change might affect residents’ sense of community.

```action
{"tool": "propose", "gene_genome": {"mechanism_genome": "Neighborhood gentrification may influence long-term residents’ sense of community through broad changes in housing stability, local amenities, neighborhood identity, and everyday social interactions.", "niche_genome": "Urban sociology research on changing neighborhoods, especially communities experiencing demographic, economic, and cultural shifts associated with gentrification.", "observation_genome": "Residents may feel less attached to a neighborhood when familiar people, places, businesses, or routines change, although some may also perceive improvements in services, safety, or infrastructure.", "limitation_genome": "The effects of gentrification are likely to vary across residents and neighborhoods, depending on factors such as income, tenure, race, age, length of residence, and the pace or form of neighborhood change.", "delta_genome": "Frame sense of community as shaped not only by whether people move away, but also by whether they continue to recognize and participate in neighborhood life.", "claim_genome": "Gentrification is most likely to weaken long-term residents’ sense of community when economic pressure and cultural change make the neighborhood feel less familiar or accessible, though attachment may persist where residents retain stable housing and meaningful local connections."}}
```

full_prompt: Propose a recycling process for lithium iron phosphate batteries building on hydrometallurgical leaching and direct cathode regeneration. Conventional leaching recovers lithium but destroys cathode morphology and can be uneconomic for low-value LFP; target regenerated cathode capacity above 95% of new material with 30% lower chemical use.
chosen:
Rationale: I’ll do one targeted confirmation that hybrid “selective Li leaching + direct LFP relithiation/regeneration” remains a credible 2025 direction for low-value LFP recycling.

```action search
{"query":"2025 lithium iron phosphate battery recycling selective lithium leaching direct regeneration relithiation capacity retention chemical consumption"}
```

[result] Recent LFP recycling literature continues to support hybrid routes: selective lithium extraction can preserve FePO4/LFP framework value better than full dissolution, while direct relithiation plus thermal repair can restore regenerated LFP to near-new electrochemical performance. Reported regenerated capacities commonly approach or exceed ~95% of commercial/new LFP when stoichiometry, carbon coating, and calcination atmosphere are controlled. Chemical-intensity reduction is plausible by avoiding full Fe/P dissolution and minimizing pH-swing precipitation.

Rationale: Propose a process gene that combines mild selective Li removal, morphology-preserving FePO4 skeleton reuse, closed-loop relithiation, and short thermal/carbon repair to meet the >95% capacity and −30% chemical-use targets.

```action propose
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "gene_genome": {
    "concept": "Hybrid hydrometallurgical-direct regeneration process for spent LFP in which lithium is selectively leached while preserving the FePO4/LFP particle framework, followed by stoichiometric relithiation, carbon repair, and low-temperature annealing to regenerate battery-grade LiFePO4.",
    "feed_preparation": "Discharge, dismantle, shred, and separate black mass; remove Al/Cu and graphite by sieving/flotation. Use low-temperature binder removal or solvent-assisted delamination to preserve secondary-particle morphology and avoid aggressive full dissolution.",
    "selective_leaching_strategy": "Apply mild organic-acid or buffered oxidizing leach targeted mainly at Li extraction, converting depleted LFP toward a FePO4-rich skeleton without dissolving most Fe and P. Recycle leach liquor and use electrodialysis or controlled crystallization to recover Li as Li2CO3/LiOH for reuse in relithiation.",
    "direct_regeneration_step": "Blend preserved FePO4/LFP skeleton with recovered lithium salt plus small stoichiometric correction excess; add glucose/sucrose or bio-derived carbon precursor; spray-dry or wet-mill to restore homogeneous Li distribution, then anneal under inert/reducing atmosphere to rebuild olivine LFP and repair conductive carbon coating.",
    "performance_targets": "Regenerated cathode should reach at least 95% of new LFP specific capacity, with target discharge capacity around 150–160 mAh/g at moderate rate, low Fe/P impurity dissolution, restored Li:Fe:P stoichiometry near 1:1:1, and cycle retention comparable to commercial LFP after formation cycling.",
    "resource_advantage": "Reduce chemical use by at least 30% versus conventional full hydrometallurgical dissolution by avoiding bulk Fe/P leaching, minimizing neutralization/precipitation reagents, recycling lithium-bearing liquor, and using direct cathode-framework repair instead of resynthesizing LFP entirely from purified salts."
  }
}
```

full_prompt: 请提出一个关于空间导航中海马体认知地图表征的实验，并显式要求 gene_genome 格式输出，包含 6 个字段。
chosen:
我先检索“海马体认知地图”“空间导航”“表征格式/实验范式”的交叉点，以便把实验问题限定在可操作的人类认知科学范式中。  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"hippocampal cognitive map representation spatial navigation experiment human virtual reality fMRI cognitive science"}
```
[result]: 检索结果显示，该领域常用虚拟现实导航、fMRI/MEG、多变量模式分析与表征相似性分析来研究海马体是否编码位置、方向、目标距离、拓扑关系或任务结构。一个可发展的空白是：区分海马体表征的是“欧氏几何地图”还是“任务相关的图结构地图”。

接着检查该构想的新颖性：把同一空间环境设计成几何距离与图结构距离相互冲突，可直接检验海马体认知地图的表征维度。  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","idea":"Use a virtual navigation task where Euclidean distance and graph/topological distance are dissociated, and test hippocampal cognitive map representations with fMRI RSA and behavioral planning measures."}
```
[result]: 该想法与现有虚拟导航和海马表征研究相关，但若明确操纵“几何距离—拓扑距离冲突”，并要求输出标准化 gene_genome 六字段实验描述，可形成一个清晰、可复现的实验提案。

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"我建议开展一项人类虚拟现实空间导航实验，研究海马体认知地图究竟更接近欧氏几何地图，还是更接近任务相关的拓扑图结构。被试将在虚拟城镇中学习若干地标位置；关键操纵是让某些地点在视觉几何上相距较近，但需要绕行多个通道才能到达，而另一些地点几何距离较远但图结构路径较短。这样可以把“物理空间距离”和“可达性/路径结构距离”分离。\n\n实验中，被试先进行自由探索和目标导航训练，随后在fMRI中完成两类任务：一类是判断两个地标的直线方向或距离，另一类是规划从一个地标到另一个地标的最短可行路径。研究者将记录反应时、路径选择、导航误差，并使用海马体及内嗅皮层的多变量模式分析和表征相似性分析，比较神经表征矩阵与欧氏距离模型、拓扑距离模型、目标价值模型之间的拟合程度。\n\n核心预测是：如果海马体主要编码几何认知地图，那么海马活动模式相似性应随欧氏距离增加而下降，即使实际路径需要绕行；如果海马体主要编码任务相关图结构，那么活动模式应更好地反映通道连接数、最短路径长度和决策节点结构。进一步可检验前海马是否偏向粗粒度任务图，后海马是否偏向精细几何位置，从而解释人类空间导航中“地图式记忆”和“路线式规划”的分工。\n\n请将该实验设计显式输出为 gene_genome 格式，并且只包含以下6个字段：gene_id、hypothesis、experimental_design、neural_measure、behavioral_measure、predicted_result。其中 gene_id 可写为 cogmap_hippocampus_nav_001；hypothesis 描述海马体表征欧氏几何距离还是拓扑路径距离；experimental_design 描述虚拟现实环境和距离冲突操纵；neural_measure 描述fMRI与RSA/MVPA；behavioral_measure 描述导航反应时、路径长度和误差；predicted_result 描述区分几何地图与图结构地图的预期结果。"}
```

full_prompt: Propose a computer vision research project for detecting rare defects in industrial inspection images using few-shot or self-supervised learning. Describe the implementation plan, including data collection, model training, augmentation strategy, evaluation protocol, and deployment considerations.
chosen:
Rationale: I first identify established industrial anomaly/defect benchmarks and current strong baselines for rare-defect detection under limited labels.

```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "few-shot self-supervised industrial anomaly detection MVTec AD VisA PatchCore DINO industrial inspection rare defects"}
```

[result]: Found relevant benchmark and method nodes: MVTec AD, VisA, DAGM, KolektorSDD2; strong baselines include PatchCore, PaDiM, FastFlow, CFA, DRAEM, RegAD, WinCLIP, DINOv2 feature extraction, masked image modeling, and contrastive self-supervised pretraining. MVTec AD and VisA report image-level AUROC, pixel-level AUROC, PRO/AUPRO, and F1-max.

Rationale: I read representative benchmark/method details to shape a concrete project around few-shot defect labels and unlabeled normal factory images.

```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_or_resource": "MVTec AD benchmark and PatchCore/DINOv2 anomaly detection papers"}
```

[result]: MVTec AD contains 15 industrial categories with defect segmentation masks and very few defective examples. PatchCore uses memory-bank nearest-neighbor scoring over pretrained CNN patch embeddings. DINOv2/ViT features improve semantic patch representations without supervised defect labels. Evaluation commonly separates normal-only training from defective test images and reports image-level AUROC, pixel-level AUROC, and AUPRO.

Rationale: I check novelty by combining self-supervised visual features, few-shot defect prototypes, synthetic defect generation, and edge-aware deployment into a single practical inspection pipeline.

```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "idea": "DINOv2-based few-shot industrial anomaly detection using normal-image self-supervised adaptation, synthetic defect augmentation, prototype-guided patch scoring, and TensorRT deployment"}
```

[result]: The combination is plausible and incremental but concrete: DINOv2/ViT patch features, normal-only adaptation, few-shot defect prototype calibration, CutPaste/Perlin/Poisson synthetic defects, and edge deployment are individually known, but a unified protocol targeted at rare industrial defects on MVTec AD and VisA is a valid applied research project.

Rationale: Based on the survey, I propose a deployable few-shot/self-supervised inspection system with explicit data, training, augmentation, evaluation, and deployment steps.

full_prompt: In a patient with early-onset colorectal cancer and a strong family history of Lynch syndrome, identify the most clinically relevant gene to evaluate, summarize its disease association, and note key implications for screening and management. Respond only as gene_genome.
chosen:
Clinical priority is to confirm a Lynch-syndrome mismatch-repair gene; MLH1 is a high-penetrance, clinically actionable candidate in early-onset colorectal cancer with strong Lynch family history.
```action search {"query":"MLH1 Lynch syndrome early onset colorectal cancer screening management GeneReviews NCCN mismatch repair"} ```
[result]: High-confidence sources identify germline pathogenic variants in DNA mismatch-repair genes—especially MLH1 and MSH2—as major causes of Lynch syndrome, with elevated colorectal and endometrial cancer risk and recommendations for intensified colonoscopic surveillance.

Extract the structured clinical gene profile focusing on disease mechanism, phenotype, and management relevance.
```action extract_genome {"gene":"MLH1","discipline":"clinical_medicine","disease_context":"early-onset colorectal cancer with strong family history of Lynch syndrome"} ```
[result]: MLH1 encodes a DNA mismatch-repair protein. Germline loss-of-function variants cause Lynch syndrome with microsatellite instability, early-onset colorectal cancer, and increased extracolonic cancer risks. Management includes genetic counseling/testing, cascade testing, frequent colonoscopy, consideration of endometrial/ovarian risk management, and tumor testing with MSI/IHC plus MLH1 methylation/BRAF assessment when MLH1 protein loss is seen.

{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"MLH1 is a DNA mismatch-repair gene; pathogenic germline variants impair repair of replication errors, causing microsatellite instability and accumulation of oncogenic mutations.","niche_genome":"Most relevant in suspected Lynch syndrome presenting as early-onset colorectal cancer with a strong autosomal-dominant family history of colorectal and related cancers.","observation_genome":"MLH1-associated Lynch syndrome confers high colorectal cancer risk and increased risk of endometrial, ovarian, gastric, urinary tract, and other cancers; tumors often show MSI-high status and loss of MLH1/PMS2 on IHC.","limitation_genome":"Isolated MLH1/PMS2 loss in a colorectal tumor can be sporadic from MLH1 promoter methylation, so germline interpretation should include tumor methylation and/or BRAF testing plus genetic counseling.","delta_genome":"Evaluation should move from tumor MSI/IHC to targeted germline testing of MLH1 and other Lynch genes, with cascade testing for relatives if a pathogenic variant is found.","claim_genome":"MLH1 is the key clinically actionable gene to evaluate in this scenario because confirming MLH1-related Lynch syndrome changes surveillance, family testing, and risk-reduction management, including colonoscopy every 1–2 years starting young or immediately after diagnosis."}}

full_prompt: Design a proposal for predicting QT prolongation using CiPA in silico models and concentration-QTc analysis. CiPA models require ion-channel panels that are unavailable early, while concentration-QTc analysis is late-stage and clinically expensive. Target an early-stage triage method that predicts QT risk from limited hERG, Nav1.5, Cav1.2, and physicochemical data.

GAIL and AIRL learn policies from demonstrations without explicit rewards. In robotics control, they can be sample-inefficient and unstable when demonstrations are few or suboptimal. Propose a reward-learning method for mobile manipulation that works with 10 demonstrations and improves task completion over behavior cloning.
chosen:
Rationale: A good fit is a few-shot reward learner that avoids adversarial instability by learning a dense, goal-conditioned reward from demonstrations using contrastive ranking plus conservative negatives, then trains the robot policy with offline-to-online RL. I will confirm that contrastive/preference-style reward learning is still a strong direction for low-demo robotic imitation.

```action search
{"query":"2025 robotics imitation learning few demonstrations contrastive reward learning improves over behavior cloning"}
```

[Result] Recent robotics imitation work continues to report that pure behavior cloning degrades with few or imperfect demonstrations, while learned dense rewards from preferences, rankings, contrastive objectives, or goal-conditioned representations can improve robustness and enable RL fine-tuning. This supports using a non-adversarial reward model rather than GAIL/AIRL for 10-demo mobile manipulation.

Rationale: Propose a method that learns progress-aware rewards from only 10 demos by combining temporal contrastive learning, hindsight negatives, and uncertainty penalties, then uses the reward for conservative offline RL followed by limited real-robot fine-tuning.

```action propose
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "gene_genome": {
    "name": "FRESH-MM: Few-shot Reward Estimation with Sequence-contrastive Hindsight for Mobile Manipulation",
    "core_idea": "Learn a dense task-progress reward from 10 demonstrations by training a goal-conditioned reward model to rank later demonstration states above earlier states, demonstrated success states above failed or perturbed states, and feasible progress above out-of-distribution behavior, then optimize a mobile-manipulation policy with conservative offline-to-online RL.",
    "method": "Encode RGB-D observations, proprioception, base pose, gripper state, and language/task goal into a shared representation. Train reward r(s,a,g) with three losses: temporal contrastive ranking within each demo, success-vs-hindsight-negative classification using automatically generated failed suffixes and object-displacement perturbations, and uncertainty-penalized reward calibration via an ensemble. Use the learned reward with conservative Q-learning or IQL on demo plus replay data, then perform short online SAC-style fine-tuning with reward uncertainty limiting unsafe exploration.",
    "why_better_than_behavior_cloning": "Behavior cloning only imitates the 10 trajectories and compounds errors when the mobile base, arm, or object pose deviates from the demo distribution. FRESH-MM learns a reusable notion of task progress, so the policy can recover from off-demo states, exploit suboptimal demonstrations by preferring later successful outcomes over earlier inefficient motions, and improve through RL rather than being capped by demonstrator quality.",
    "evaluation": "Test on mobile pick-place, drawer opening, object handoff, and fetch-and-place tasks using exactly 10 demonstrations per task. Compare against behavior cloning, diffusion policy from 10 demos, GAIL/AIRL, and sparse-reward RL. Primary metric is task completion rate; secondary metrics are recovery rate after perturbation, collision rate, path efficiency, and number of online environment interactions.",
    "expected_outcome": "The method should outperform behavior cloning in task completion under pose perturbations and imperfect demonstrations, especially on long-horizon tasks requiring base-arm coordination, while being more stable than adversarial imitation because the reward is trained with supervised contrastive and ranking objectives rather than discriminator-policy minimax updates."
  }
}
```


[Result] Recent robotics imitation work continues to report that pure behavior cloning degrades with few or imperfect demonstrations, while learned dense rewards from preferences, rankings, contrastive objectives, or goal-conditioned representations can improve robustness and enable RL fine-tuning. This supports using a non-adversarial reward model rather than GAIL/AIRL for 10-demo mobile manipulation.

Rationale: Propose a method that learns progress-aware rewards from only 10 demos by combining temporal contrastive learning, hindsight negatives, and uncertainty penalties, then uses the reward for conservative offline RL followed by limited real-robot fine-tuning.

```action propose
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "gene_genome": "FRESH-MM: Few-shot Reward Estimation with Sequence-contrastive Hindsight for Mobile Manipulation. Core idea: Learn a dense task-progress reward from 10 demonstrations by training a goal-conditioned reward model to rank later demonstration states above earlier states, demonstrated success states above failed or perturbed states, and feasible progress above out-of-distribution behavior, then optimize a mobile-manipulation policy with conservative offline-to-online RL. Method: Encode RGB-D observations, proprioception, base pose, gripper state, and language/task goal into a shared representation. Train reward r(s,a,g) with three losses: temporal contrastive ranking within each demo, success-vs-hindsight-negative classification using automatically generated failed suffixes and object-displacement perturbations, and uncertainty-penalized reward calibration via an ensemble. Use the learned reward with conservative Q-learning or IQL on demo plus replay data, then perform short online SAC-style fine-tuning with reward uncertainty limiting unsafe exploration. Why better than behavior cloning: Behavior cloning only imitates the 10 trajectories and compounds errors when the mobile base, arm, or object pose deviates from the demo distribution. FRESH-MM learns a reusable notion of task progress, so the policy can recover from off-demo states, exploit suboptimal demonstrations by preferring later successful outcomes over earlier inefficient motions, and improve through RL rather than being capped by demonstrator quality. Evaluation: Test on mobile pick-place, drawer opening, object handoff, and fetch-and-place tasks using exactly 10 demonstrations per task. Compare against behavior cloning, diffusion policy from 10 demos, GAIL/AIRL, and sparse-reward RL. Primary metric is task completion rate; secondary metrics are recovery rate after perturbation, collision rate, path efficiency, and number of online environment interactions. Expected outcome: The method should outperform behavior cloning in task completion under pose perturbations and imperfect demonstrations, especially on long-horizon tasks requiring base-arm coordination, while being more stable than adversarial imitation because the reward is trained with supervised contrastive and ranking objectives rather than discriminator-policy minimax updates."
}
```

full_prompt: Propose a project to create radiation-tolerant materials for next-generation nuclear reactors, with an implementation plan involving candidate selection, ion irradiation experiments, defect characterization, high-temperature mechanical testing, and failure analysis.
chosen:
I will ground the project in known radiation-damage strategies for advanced reactor structural materials, then convert the evidence into a concrete experimental plan.
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"radiation tolerant structural materials next generation nuclear reactors ODS steels high entropy alloys ion irradiation defect characterization high temperature mechanical testing"}
```
[result]: Simulated search result: Recent literature identifies oxide-dispersion-strengthened ferritic/martensitic steels, refractory high-entropy alloys, SiC/SiC composites, and MAX-phase ceramics as leading radiation-tolerant candidates for Gen-IV reactors. Common workflows use CALPHAD/DFT screening, heavy-ion irradiation to 10–100 dpa, TEM/APT/PAS defect analysis, nanoindentation and micro-tensile testing, followed by fracture and failure analysis at 600–900 °C.

A focused read is needed to identify practical candidate classes and measurable success criteria for an implementable materials project.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper":"Radiation tolerance of high entropy alloys and oxide dispersion strengthened steels for advanced nuclear energy systems"}
```
[result]: Simulated read result: Key findings: nanometer-scale oxide particles in ODS steels act as defect sinks and stabilize microstructure at high temperature; refractory HEAs such as W-Ta-Cr-V and Mo-Nb-Ta-Ti show sluggish defect evolution and reduced void swelling under ion irradiation; radiation tolerance should be assessed using swelling, hardening, defect cluster density, phase stability, helium bubble density, creep strength, and fracture mode after irradiation.

Expected outcome: identify at least two candidate alloys, expected to be one ODS ferritic/martensitic steel and one refractory high-entropy alloy, that show less than 1% swelling, less than 20% irradiation hardening, and at least 80% strength retention at 700 °C after 50 dpa, making them viable lead materials for neutron-irradiation validation."}}

full_prompt:
Research topic: Develop retrieval-augmented generation methods that reduce hallucination in domain-specific large language models by dynamically verifying cited evidence against structured knowledge bases.

Discipline: computer_science

Propose a novel follow-up research idea building on relevant prior work.
chosen:
I’ll start broadly with hallucination reduction in RAG, then progressively narrow toward citation-grounded verification and structured-KB validation for domain-specific LLMs.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"retrieval augmented generation hallucination reduction evidence verification domain specific large language models","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025,"k":5}
```

[result]: The search surfaces foundational RAG work such as Lewis et al., “Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks” — OpenAlex `W3090951108` — plus newer hallucination-mitigation methods including Self-RAG, RARR, and citation-grounded QA benchmarks. The broad pattern is that retrieval improves factuality, but many systems still trust retrieved text implicitly and do not verify whether generated claims are actually supported by cited evidence. Structured knowledge bases appear mostly in KG-RAG work, but less often as dynamic citation verifiers.

The broad search suggests the key gap is not “more retrieval,” but whether each generated, cited claim can be checked against external evidence. I’ll narrow to systems that explicitly generate citations or revise unsupported claims.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"LLM generated citations evidence attribution hallucination verification RAG ALCE RARR Self-RAG","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025,"k":5}
```

[result]: Relevant papers include Asai et al., “Self-RAG: Learning to Retrieve, Generate, and Critique through Self-Reflection” — OpenAlex `W4387897117`; Gao et al., “RARR: Researching and Revising What Language Models Say, Using Language Models” — OpenAlex `W4311291062`; and Gao et al., “Enabling Large Language Models to Generate Text with Citations” / ALCE — OpenAlex `W4386059530`. These methods introduce citation or critique loops, but verification is largely text-passage-based, often using LLM/NLI judgments rather than schema-aware KB consistency checks. The promising direction is claim-level attribution plus an external verifier that is not just another unconstrained LLM.

Now I’ll narrow further to structured knowledge bases and knowledge-graph RAG, because the user specifically asks for verification against structured KBs rather than only retrieved passages.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"knowledge graph retrieval augmented generation structured knowledge base fact verification citation grounding LLM hallucination","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025,"k":5}
```

[result]: The search finds KG-RAG and graph-grounded QA work using Wikidata, biomedical ontologies, UMLS, and domain KGs to improve factuality. However, most systems use KGs as retrieval context or prompt scaffolding, not as a dynamic verifier of the citations produced by the model. Few methods create a machine-checkable proof linking a generated sentence, its citation, extracted triples, and KB constraints such as entity type, relation validity, temporal scope, or contradiction.

Self-RAG is central because it makes retrieval and critique part of generation rather than a static preprocessing step. I’ll read it to identify what its verifier can and cannot do.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"W4387897117"}
```

[result]: Self-RAG trains a model to decide when to retrieve, generate responses, and critique its own outputs using reflection tokens for relevance, support, and utility. Its strength is dynamic retrieval and self-assessment during generation, rather than always retrieving blindly. Its limitation for this topic is that support checking remains largely text- and model-judgment-based; it does not validate cited claims against a structured KB with explicit entity linking, relation constraints, or auditable proof objects.

ALCE is important because it focuses on long-form answers with citations. I’ll read it to understand current citation-level evaluation and where structured verification could extend it.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"W4386059530"}
```

[result]: ALCE studies whether LLMs can produce long-form answers with citations that actually support their claims, using retrieval and citation evaluation metrics such as citation recall and citation precision. It shows that citation presence is not equivalent to citation faithfulness: models may cite irrelevant or insufficient passages. The limitation is that citation correctness is evaluated mainly through textual support, not by checking generated claims against structured domain KBs, ontological constraints, or contradictory database entries.

The concrete opportunity is now clearer: combine Self-RAG-style dynamic retrieval/critique with ALCE-style citation grounding, but add a structured verifier that checks every cited claim against a domain KB. I’ll run a novelty check around this exact mechanism.

```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","mechanism":"During RAG decoding, convert each cited sentence into normalized claim triples, entity-link them to a domain knowledge base, verify citation support and KB consistency using schema constraints plus textual entailment, then force repair, abstention, or citation replacement before final output.","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025}
```

[result]: Adjacent work exists in Self-RAG, RARR, ALCE, KG-RAG, and automated fact-checking, but the precise mechanism of decoding-time citation verification against structured KB constraints appears underexplored. Prior systems usually retrieve graph facts before generation, evaluate citations post hoc, or use LLM/NLI critics without symbolic consistency checks. The novel niche is an auditable “citation-to-KB proof loop” for domain-specific LLMs.

```action
{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"Develop Verify-Then-Cite RAG: a generation-time controller that decomposes each draft sentence with citations into atomic claims, maps entities and relations to a structured domain KB, checks whether the cited passage and KB jointly entail the claim, and either accepts, repairs, replaces the citation, or abstains. The verifier combines entity linking, relation/schema validation, temporal consistency checks, KB contradiction detection, and lightweight NLI over the cited passage.","niche_genome":"Domain-specific LLMs in high-stakes settings where structured KBs already exist, especially biomedical QA using UMLS/SNOMED CT/DrugBank, legal QA using statute and case-law graphs, or enterprise technical support using product ontologies. Initial experiments should use biomedical long-form QA because hallucinated relations such as drug–disease, gene–phenotype, or contraindication claims can be validated against structured resources.","observation_genome":"RAG reduces hallucination but does not guarantee that a generated citation supports the exact sentence it is attached to. Self-RAG shows that retrieval and critique can be dynamic, while ALCE shows that citation faithfulness is measurable but fragile. KG-RAG shows that structured knowledge can improve factuality, yet most work uses KGs as context rather than as citation-level verifiers.","limitation_genome":"Current citation-grounded RAG systems rely heavily on retrieved text and LLM/NLI judgments, making them vulnerable to plausible but unsupported citations. Current KG-RAG systems often retrieve structured facts before generation but do not enforce that every final cited claim is KB-consistent. Post-hoc fact-checking also arrives too late: the model may already have committed to an unsupported answer style or misleading citation set.","delta_genome":"The key change is to make structured verification part of the decoding and revision loop, not merely retrieval or post-hoc evaluation. Each answer sentence receives a machine-checkable evidence object: cited passage span, extracted claim triple, linked KB entities, relation path or constraint, entailment score, contradiction flag, and repair decision. This creates citation-level provenance that can be audited and used as a training signal for future domain-specific LLMs.","claim_genome":"Hypothesis: compared with standard RAG, Self-RAG-style retrieval, and citation-only baselines, Verify-Then-Cite RAG will significantly reduce unsupported citations and domain-specific factual hallucinations while preserving answer usefulness. The strongest measurable gains should appear on relation-heavy questions where textual retrieval alone is ambiguous but structured KB constraints can rule out incorrect entity relations, outdated facts, or impossible type combinations."}}
```

The project directly builds on Self-RAG’s dynamic retrieve-and-critique loop and ALCE’s citation-faithfulness framing, but adds structured, auditable KB-grounded verification as a first-class generation constraint.
